proposals.title: Systematic ab initio calculation of relativistic, QED, and diamagnetic contributions to molecular hyperfine structure constants for heavy p-block elements
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 high-precision molecular spectroscopy and hyperfine structure (HFS) studies, crucial for determining nuclear quadrupole moments (NQMs) and testing fundamental physics, are limited by incomplete theoretical models. Standard calculations often exclude subtle but non-negligible physical contributions like higher-order relativistic effects, quantum electrodynamics (QED) corrections, and diamagnetic shielding, introducing systematic errors in extracted nuclear properties and electronic structure factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic inclusion of these neglected contributions—specifically the Breit interaction, one-loop QED self-energy and vacuum polarization, and the diamagnetic shielding term—in state-of-the-art relativistic coupled-cluster calculations will significantly improve the agreement between theoretical and experimental HFS constants for heavy p-block molecules like BiF and SbN, leading to more accurate and reliable determinations of their nuclear quadrupole moments.
proposals.method: We will employ a four-component relativistic Dirac-Coulomb Hamiltonian as the foundation, implemented within the DIRAC code package. The electronic wavefunctions for target molecules (e.g., BiF, SbN) will be generated using the coupled-cluster singles, doubles, and perturbative triples [CCSD(T)] method. On this baseline, we will sequentially compute corrections: the frequency-independent Breit interaction, the one-loop QED corrections using an effective nuclear potential model, and the diamagnetic shielding contribution to the magnetic HFS. The finite nuclear size will be modeled with Gaussian charge distributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of these higher-order effects for molecular HFS in heavy p-block systems, quantifying their individual and combined impact. It will deliver a set of refined, 'theory-corrected' HFS constants, enabling a subsequent re-determination of the NQMs for Bi and Sb with reduced systematic uncertainty, advancing the field of precision nuclear property determination from molecular data.
proposals.novelty: While these contributions are studied for atomic systems, their concerted evaluation and application to molecular hyperfine structure—particularly for extracting nuclear moments—is novel. The project moves beyond the standard relativistic coupled-cluster paradigm by integrating QED and diamagnetic terms directly into the molecular HFS workflow for heavy elements.

proposals.title: Relativistic Coupled-Cluster and Configuration Interaction Methods for High-Accuracy Hyperfine Structure in Open-Shell p-Block Atoms
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the benchmark accuracy of relativistic quantum chemistry for properties like hyperfine structure and nuclear quadrupole moments is largely confined to one- and two-electron systems (e.g., H, He-like ions). For heavier many-electron atoms, which are critical for interpreting atomic parity violation experiments and extracting fundamental nuclear properties from spectroscopic data, achieving sub-percent accuracy remains a formidable challenge. Extending this precision to open-shell p-block atoms like Sn, Pb, or Bi would bridge atomic physics, nuclear physics, and fundamental symmetry tests.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically combining relativistic Fock-space coupled-cluster (FSCC) and multireference configuration interaction (MRCI) methods with high-order correlation treatments and careful basis set construction, it is possible to calculate electronic factors for hyperfine constants in selected 3- and 4-electron valence atoms with an accuracy rivaling that of two-electron systems.
proposals.method: The project will employ a dual-methodology approach. First, we will use the relativistic FSCC method, starting from a closed-shell Dirac-Hartree-Fock reference (e.g., Sn²⁺ for neutral Sn), to treat electron correlation for the valence electrons. Second, for states where FSCC struggles, we will implement a relativistic MRCI approach using optimized, correlation-consistent basis sets (Dyall's or similar) and include core-polarization and pair-correlation effects via perturbative corrections. The electronic wavefunctions will be used to compute the A and B hyperfine constants for selected states in Sn I, Pb I, and Bi II, comparing directly against the most precise experimental data available pre-2026.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new set of benchmark-quality electronic structure calculations for open-shell, many-electron heavy atoms, providing the most reliable ab initio hyperfine factors to date. This will enable the extraction of nuclear quadrupole moments for isotopes like ¹²³Sb and ²⁰⁹Bi with reduced uncertainty and establish a methodological pathway for precision studies in atoms relevant to the search for new physics beyond the Standard Model.
proposals.novelty: While FSCC and MRCI are known, their synergistic application and systematic push towards sub-1% accuracy for hyperfine constants in atoms with complex open-shell structures (beyond alkali-like systems) is novel. The explicit targeting of p-block atoms, where relativistic and correlation effects are intricately coupled, moves beyond the traditional two-electron frontier.

proposals.title: Determining Nuclear Quadrupole Moments of Krypton and Xenon Isotopes from Hyperfine Structure in Diatomic Interhalogen Molecules
proposals.motivation: Accurate nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) are fundamental for testing nuclear models and interpreting hyperfine interactions in atoms and molecules. For heavy, neutron-rich isotopes of noble gases like Kr and Xe, experimental Q values are often poorly known or rely on atomic data with significant electronic structure uncertainties. Molecular spectroscopy offers a precise alternative, as the electric field gradient at the nucleus can be calculated with high accuracy using relativistic quantum chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: The nuclear quadrupole moments of selected krypton and xenon isotopes can be determined with improved accuracy by combining experimental molecular hyperfine constants for KrF, XeF, and related interhalogens with state-of-the-art relativistic coupled-cluster calculations of the electric field gradient.
proposals.method: We will first compile and critically assess existing microwave and laser spectroscopic data on hyperfine structure for isotopologues of KrF, XeF, and potentially KrCl/XeCl. For each system, we will perform relativistic four-component Dirac-Coulomb coupled-cluster calculations with single, double, and perturbative triple excitations (CCSD(T)) to compute the electric field gradient (q) at the Kr/Xe nucleus. The nuclear quadrupole moment Q will then be extracted from the experimental coupling constant B = (eQq)/h. The computational protocol will include systematic studies of basis set convergence, correlation effects, and the impact of the negative ion charge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new set of benchmark nuclear quadrupole moments for krypton and xenon isotopes, reducing current uncertainties. It will demonstrate the broader applicability of the molecular method beyond simple hydrides like HD, establishing a reliable protocol for heavy, closed-shell atoms. The results will serve as critical input for nuclear structure studies and for calibrating atomic calculations of hyperfine structure.
proposals.novelty: While the method has been applied to light systems and some heavy p-block elements, its systematic application to extract Q moments from heavy noble gas atoms within chemically bonded, polar molecules is novel. This leverages the unique electronic structure of interhalogens to probe nuclei that are otherwise inaccessible in stable atomic states.

proposals.title: QED Corrections to Hyperfine Structure in Heavy Diatomics: A Path to Refined Nuclear Quadrupole Moments for Sb and Bi
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the most precise determinations of nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) for heavy elements like Sb and Bi rely on molecular hyperfine structure, but are limited by the accuracy of electronic structure factors. The dominant uncertainty now lies in uncalculated quantum electrodynamics (QED) effects, such as vacuum polarization and self-energy, which are significant for high-Z atoms but largely unexplored in molecular binding environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating leading-order QED corrections into relativistic coupled-cluster calculations of electric field gradients (EFGs) in heavy diatomic molecules (e.g., SbF, BiF) will systematically reduce the uncertainty in extracted nuclear quadrupole moments, resolving discrepancies between values obtained from different molecular systems.
proposals.method: We will employ the four-component relativistic Dirac-Coulomb-Breit Hamiltonian as the baseline, using the DIRAC code for accurate molecular wavefunctions. QED corrections will be introduced via the model Lamb-shift operator within the no-pair approximation, treating vacuum polarization with Uehling potential and self-energy with an effective local potential. These corrections will be implemented as perturbations to high-precision coupled-cluster singles, doubles, and perturbative triples [CCSD(T)] calculations of EFGs for selected states of Sb- and Bi-containing molecules, directly linking to our recent work on determining Q from molecular data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic assessment of molecular QED effects on hyperfine structure constants for heavy p-block elements. It will yield a new generation of benchmark nuclear quadrupole moments for Sb and Bi with significantly reduced uncertainties, crucial for nuclear structure studies and for testing fundamental symmetries in heavy-atom systems.
proposals.novelty: While QED methods are standard in atomic physics for high-Z ions, their application to bound molecular systems, where electron correlation and bonding effects are paramount, is a novel and non-trivial extension that bridges particle physics techniques with molecular quantum chemistry.

proposals.title: Relativistic Four-Component DFT for Metalloprotein Active Sites: A Fragment-Based Approach to Spin-Orbit Coupling and NMR Shielding in Bismuth-Containing Complexes
proposals.motivation: The biological activity of metalloproteins containing heavy elements (e.g., Bi in antimicrobials, Pt in chemotherapeutics) is governed by electronic structure details where scalar relativistic effects and spin-orbit coupling (SOC) are significant. Pre-2026, full relativistic quantum chemical treatment of entire biomolecules is computationally prohibitive, limiting our understanding of how relativistic effects modulate properties like NMR chemical shifts, redox potentials, and ligand binding.
proposals.hypothesis: The relativistic electronic structure, particularly SOC and NMR shielding, of a heavy-atom core in a large biological complex can be accurately modeled by a four-component Density Functional Theory (4c-DFT) calculation on a chemically meaningful fragment, with the electrostatic and polarization effects of the protein environment incorporated via a point-charge embedding scheme.
proposals.method: We will develop a fragment-based relativistic workflow. First, a large metalloprotein (e.g., a bismuth-bound protein) will be partitioned using the Huzinaga-Cantu 'atoms-in-molecules' scheme to define a core fragment containing the heavy metal and its first coordination shell. This fragment will be treated with 4c-DFT using the DIRAC code, employing the Dirac-Coulomb Hamiltonian and suitable relativistic basis sets. The long-range electrostatic potential of the remaining protein will be represented by point charges derived from a classical force field. Key properties—electric field gradients (for nuclear quadrupole moments), NMR shielding tensors of the heavy nucleus and nearby light atoms, and SOC corrections to excitation energies—will be calculated for the embedded fragment and compared to non-relativistic and scalar relativistic results.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first practical protocol for obtaining fully relativistic property predictions for heavy elements in biologically relevant molecular environments. It will deliver benchmark-quality NMR parameters for Bi in a protein context, aiding spectroscopic interpretation, and quantify the direct and indirect (via ligand polarization) contributions of SOC to electronic structure in these systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted integration of Pyykkö's expertise in precise relativistic property calculations (e.g., nuclear quadrupole moments) with a fragment-based embedding strategy, scaling 4c methods to systems an order of magnitude larger than previously possible, specifically for biologically critical heavy elements beyond the typical 5d series.

proposals.title: Statistical Shape Analysis of Atomic Electron Densities: Deriving New Radii for Nuclear Quadrupole Moment Determination
proposals.motivation: The accurate determination of nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) from molecular or atomic hyperfine data requires precise knowledge of the electric field gradient (EFG) at the nucleus, which is critically dependent on the electron density distribution. Traditional atomic radii are empirical and lack a direct, quantitative link to the shape parameters of the calculated electron density. A more rigorous, shape-derived radius could significantly improve the accuracy of extracted Q values, a core challenge in nuclear and atomic physics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that statistical shape parameters (e.g., principal moments, eccentricity) of ab initio calculated electron densities can be used to define a physically meaningful, element-specific 'quadrupole radius' (R_Q). This R_Q will provide a more accurate and systematic scaling factor for converting measured EFGs into nuclear quadrupole moments than currently used empirical radii.
proposals.method: We will perform high-level relativistic coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) or density functional theory (DFT) calculations to obtain accurate electron densities for a series of atoms, particularly p-block elements like Sb, Bi, and Sn. The 3D electron density distribution will be analyzed using statistical shape descriptors: we will compute its inertia tensor, derive its principal moments and axes, and calculate shape metrics like asphericity and prolateness. A novel 'quadrupole radius' R_Q will be defined from these parameters, likely related to the root-mean-square radial extent along the principal axes of the density. This R_Q will then be applied to re-analyze existing hyperfine data to extract refined nuclear quadrupole moments.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-principles, reproducible method for defining atomic radii directly from electron density shape, moving beyond tabulated empirical values. It will provide a new set of benchmark quadrupole moments for key isotopes (e.g., 121Sb, 123Sb, 209Bi), reducing systematic errors in nuclear structure databases. The methodology will create a direct bridge between computational electronic structure analysis and experimental nuclear observables.
proposals.novelty: While electron density analysis is common, applying formal statistical shape analysis to define a new class of atomic radii for nuclear property determination is novel. This approach quantitatively connects the anisotropy of the electron cloud—a key factor for EFG—to a scalar radius used in the extraction of Q, a link not made in prior work.

proposals.title: Determining Nuclear Quadrupole Moments of Heavy p-Block Elements via High-Resolution Spectroscopy of Cold Diatomic Cations
proposals.motivation: Accurate nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) are fundamental for interpreting hyperfine structure and testing nuclear models, but values for many heavy elements, particularly post-transition metals and metalloids like Tl, Pb, and Bi, remain uncertain or rely on semi-empirical estimates. Gas-phase spectroscopy of dimers provides a pristine environment to extract precise molecular electric field gradients, which, combined with high-level electronic structure calculations, offers a direct path to benchmark Q values. This addresses a core challenge in atomic and nuclear physics where solid-state effects or complex electronic correlations often obscure the nuclear parameter.
proposals.hypothesis: The nuclear quadrupole moments of selected heavy p-block elements (e.g., 205Tl, 209Bi) can be determined with unprecedented accuracy by measuring and analyzing the hyperfine structure of their homonuclear diatomic cations (e.g., Tl2+, Bi2+) formed and cooled in an ion trap.
proposals.method: We will employ a cryogenic linear Paul trap coupled to a laser ablation source to produce cold (T < 10 K) ensembles of diatomic cations like Bi2+. High-resolution laser spectroscopy, specifically velocity-modulation or action spectroscopy in the near-infrared to visible range, will be used to resolve the hyperfine structure of suitable electronic transitions. The experimental electric field gradient (q) at the nucleus will be derived from the measured hyperfine splittings. This q will be compared to state-of-the-art relativistic coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) calculations with quadruple-zeta basis sets and explicit treatment of electron correlation, using Pyykkö's established methodologies, to back-calculate the nuclear quadrupole moment Q.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide benchmark nuclear quadrupole moments for 2-3 heavy elements, reducing current uncertainties by an order of magnitude. It will deliver a validated, high-precision combined experimental-computational protocol for determining Q from molecular ions, applicable to other systems. The results will critically test advanced nuclear structure calculations and improve the interpretation of hyperfine data across physics and chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While Pyykkö's group has extracted Q from neutral molecules, applying this methodology to cold, trapped diatomic cations eliminates complex intermolecular interactions and allows for deeper rotational cooling, yielding sharper spectral lines. The direct focus on the cation, rather than the neutral dimer, provides a different and potentially more straightforward electronic structure for precise q calculation.

proposals.title: Determining Electron Affinities and Band Gaps of Heavy p-Block Elements via High-Precision Coupled-Cluster and Relativistic DFT Calculations
proposals.motivation: Accurate electron affinities (EAs) and band gaps are fundamental for understanding chemical bonding, reactivity, and optoelectronic properties, yet they are notoriously difficult to measure or calculate for heavy elements due to strong relativistic and electron correlation effects. Pyykkö's recent work on nuclear quadrupole moments and hyperfine structure provides a foundation of high-precision atomic and molecular electronic structure methods, but a systematic computational approach to determine EAs and complete the electronic structure picture from ionization potential to affinity is lacking, particularly for the unique 2p and heavy 6p-block elements.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a combined coupled-cluster and relativistic density functional theory (DFT) protocol, benchmarked against known spectroscopic data, it is possible to predict with high accuracy the electron affinities and fundamental band gaps of heavy p-block elements (e.g., Sb, Bi, Po) and their small clusters, where experimental data are scarce or conflicting.
proposals.method: The project will employ a two-tiered computational strategy. First, high-level relativistic coupled-cluster calculations (e.g., CCSD(T) with Dirac-Coulomb-Breit Hamiltonian) will be performed on atoms and diatomics to obtain benchmark electron affinities, using correlation-consistent basis sets and extrapolation to the complete basis set limit. Second, these results will validate and calibrate a range of relativistic DFT functionals (including meta-GGAs and hybrids) for calculating band gaps of solid-state phases or larger clusters. The 'ionization energy method' (using total energy differences for N and N±1 electron systems) will be the primary route for EA determination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a consistent, ab initio dataset of electron affinities and band gaps for heavy p-block elements, resolving existing discrepancies in the literature. It will establish a reliable computational protocol that complements experimental techniques and extends Pyykkö's methodology from hyperfine properties to global electronic structure descriptors, directly impacting materials design and periodic table trends.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct integration of Pyykkö's expertise in precision atomic calculations for nuclear moments with the challenge of predicting electron affinities and band gaps, creating a unified electronic structure picture from the same computational foundation, which is not a standard application of these methods.

proposals.title: Systematic DFT Benchmarking and Prediction of Ionization Energies for Heavy p-Block Elements: A Bridge to Hyperfine Structure Analysis
proposals.motivation: Accurate ionization energies (IEs) are fundamental for understanding atomic structure and chemical bonding, particularly for heavy elements where relativistic effects dominate. Pre-2026, experimental IEs for many high-Z, rare, or radioactive species remain uncertain or unmeasured, creating a bottleneck for interpreting hyperfine interactions and nuclear properties derived from atomic and molecular spectra.
proposals.hypothesis: That a carefully calibrated density functional theory (DFT) approach, incorporating scalar and spin-orbit relativistic effects via effective core potentials, can predict first and successive ionization energies for heavy p-block elements (e.g., groups 13-16, periods 4-6) with chemical accuracy (±0.1 eV), providing reliable data where experiment is lacking.
proposals.method: We will employ a systematic DFT protocol using the Gaussian 16 suite. Calculations will utilize the Zeroth-Order Regular Approximation (ZORA) within the ADF code and the Dirac-Coulomb Hamiltonian via the DIRAC package for benchmark relativistic treatment. A range of exchange-correlation functionals (PBE0, B3LYP, TPSS, and the Minnesota MN15 family) will be tested on atoms and small ions (e.g., Sn, Sb, Te, Pb, Bi) with known, precise experimental IEs. The optimal functional/basis set (e.g., aug-cc-pVnZ-PP, dyall.v3z) combination will then be applied to predict IEs for poorly characterized systems, directly comparing ΔSCF and orbital eigenvalue (Koopmans' theorem) approaches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated computational protocol for IE prediction in heavy elements, filling critical gaps in atomic data tables. The predicted IEs will directly enable more accurate extraction of nuclear quadrupole moments and hyperfine structure constants from spectroscopic data, strengthening the link between Pyykkö's established work on nuclear properties and electronic structure theory.
proposals.novelty: While DFT is widely used, a systematic, relativistic benchmark focused specifically on ionization energies across the heavy p-block, with explicit feedback to nuclear moment determination, is novel. The integration of IE predictions with the analysis of hyperfine A and B constants creates a self-consistent computational-experimental loop for heavy-element characterization.

proposals.title: Targeted Design of Heavy-Element Compounds via Relativistically-Derived Nuclear Quadrupole Moment Constraints
proposals.motivation: Relativistic quantum chemistry has been highly successful in explaining the properties of heavy-element compounds, particularly hyperfine interactions. However, its predictive power for *designing* new, stable species remains underexploited. This is crucial for advancing the chemistry of superheavy elements and exotic isotopes, where synthesis is costly and stability is a major concern.
proposals.hypothesis: That accurate, relativistic ab initio calculations of nuclear quadrupole moments (NQMs) and derived molecular electric field gradients (EFGs) can be used as a design criterion to identify specific isotope-substituted heavy-element molecules with enhanced electronic stability and distinct spectroscopic signatures.
proposals.method: We will extend Pyykkö's established methodology for determining NQMs from molecular data into an inverse design protocol. Using relativistic coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) and density functional theory (DFT) with exact two-component (X2C) Hamiltonians, we will calculate EFGs for a series of candidate bismuth and antimony compounds (e.g., BiX3, SbX5, X=halogen/organic ligand). By systematically varying the central isotope (e.g., 209Bi, 123Sb, 121Sb) and ligand environment, we will identify combinations where the relativistic stabilization of specific electronic shells (e.g., 6s, 6p) synergizes with the nuclear quadrupole interaction to minimize total energy and maximize bond strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for the rational design of heavy-element compounds, moving beyond post-hoc explanation. It will yield specific, testable predictions for new, isotopically-labeled molecules with optimized stability. The results will also refine our understanding of the interplay between nuclear structure (via NQMs) and relativistic electronic effects in chemical bonding.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing the precise calculation of nuclear quadrupole moments—typically an endpoint for spectroscopic analysis—as an active *design parameter* for chemical synthesis. This inverts the traditional explanatory workflow to a predictive one.

proposals.title: Relativistic and Nuclear Quadrupole Contributions to the Hyperfine Structure and Band Topology in Bismuth-Based Topological Insulators
proposals.motivation: Topological insulators (TIs) like Bi2Se3 and Bi2Te3, discovered before 2010, host protected surface states due to strong spin-orbit coupling, a key relativistic effect. However, a quantitative, first-principles understanding of how core relativistic effects—including scalar relativity and spin-orbit coupling—interact with nuclear properties like quadrupole moments to influence hyperfine interactions and local electronic structure in these materials remains incomplete. This gap limits our ability to probe and control TI states using nuclear techniques like perturbed angular correlation spectroscopy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relativistic enhancement of p- and d-electron densities at heavy nuclei (e.g., Bi, Sb) in TIs significantly modifies the electric field gradients (EFGs), and that these EFGs, combined with precise nuclear quadrupole moments (Q), are critical for interpreting hyperfine data and may correlate with topological band inversion metrics.
proposals.method: We will employ state-of-the-art relativistic density functional theory (DFT) calculations, using a four-component Dirac-Coulomb Hamiltonian as implemented in codes like DIRAC or BERTHA, to compute EFGs at Bi/Sb sites in bulk and thin-film TI structures. The calculated EFGs will be combined with our group's recently determined, accurate nuclear quadrupole moments for 209Bi and 121,123Sb to predict quadrupole coupling constants. We will further perform detailed analyses of the electronic factors contributing to the EFG, decomposing contributions from core and valence shells, and correlate these with band inversion strengths and spin-texture characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first ab initio database of relativistic EFGs and predicted hyperfine parameters for key TI materials, directly aiding experimental characterization. It will establish a quantitative link between nuclear quadrupole resonance observables and the relativistic electronic structure underpinning topology, creating a new diagnostic tool. The project will extend Pyykkö's expertise in atomic-scale relativistic nuclear physics into the realm of quantum materials.
proposals.novelty: While relativistic band structure calculations for TIs are common, a focused, high-precision study of nuclear quadrupole interactions from a first-principles, atomic-physics perspective is novel. The explicit integration of newly determined nuclear moments with relativistic electronic structure calculations to decode material properties represents a unique synthesis of nuclear and condensed matter physics.

proposals.title: Relativistic Four-Component DFT Analysis of NMR Chemical Shifts for 6p-Block Heavy Nuclei: From Molecular Quadrupole Couplings to Isotropic Shifts
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, relativistic effects dominate NMR parameters for heavy nuclei, but systematic, high-accuracy analyses of heavy-atom NMR chemical shifts are scarce beyond a few well-studied cases like Pb or Tl. A comprehensive understanding of these shifts is crucial for interpreting NMR spectra in heavy-element chemistry, materials science, and for benchmarking quantum chemical methods. Extending Pyykkö's established methodology for extracting nuclear quadrupole moments (NQMs) from molecular data provides a unique foundation for this shift analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: By combining accurately determined NQMs from molecular quadrupole coupling constants with state-of-the-art relativistic quantum chemistry, we can decompose and accurately predict the paramagnetic and diamagnetic shielding contributions to NMR chemical shifts for a series of heavy 6p-block elements (e.g., Bi, Sb, Po, At), where both scalar and spin-orbit relativistic effects are significant.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step computational protocol. First, for target diatomics (e.g., BiN, SbN), we will perform four-component relativistic DFT calculations (using DIRAC with suitable functionals) to compute electric field gradients (EFGs) and magnetic shielding tensors. Second, using experimentally measured nuclear quadrupole coupling constants for these molecules, we will refine the NQMs for the heavy nuclei via the relationship NQC = (eQ)(V_zz)/h, following Pyykkö's established procedure. These refined, molecule-independent NQMs will then be used to validate the EFG calculations, ensuring the electronic structure description is accurate. Finally, the validated relativistic wavefunctions will be used to compute and analyze the isotropic NMR chemical shifts and their tensor components for these heavy nuclei in a series of molecular environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a consistent set of refined NQMs and benchmark-quality NMR chemical shift predictions for heavy 6p-block elements, filling a significant gap in spectroscopic reference data. It will establish a robust protocol for separating experimental and computational uncertainties in heavy-atom NMR, advancing the quantitative interpretation of NMR in inorganic and organometallic chemistry involving very heavy elements.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct coupling of the NQM-determination methodology—a strength of Pyykkö's group—with the full relativistic calculation of NMR shifts, creating a self-consistent framework for heavy nuclei where both electric and magnetic hyperfine properties are addressed simultaneously and with high precision.

proposals.title: Nuclear Configuration Mixing Effects on Hyperfine Anomalies: A Combined Nuclear Shell Model and Relativistic Electronic Structure Study for Heavy Elements
proposals.motivation: The extraction of nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) and the interpretation of hyperfine structure (HFS) constants from atomic and molecular data rely critically on the assumption of a pure, single-configuration nuclear state (e.g., a pure d3/2 proton state for 209Bi). This assumption neglects configuration mixing within the nuclear shell model, which can induce significant hyperfine anomalies—discrepancies between moments derived from different isotopes or measurement techniques. Resolving these anomalies is essential for achieving sub-1% accuracy in fundamental nuclear parameters and for testing atomic many-body methods in heavy systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicit inclusion of nuclear configuration mixing, particularly for odd-proton nuclei in the Z > 50 region, will quantitatively explain observed hyperfine anomalies and lead to revised, more consistent values for nuclear quadrupole moments.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step, iterative ab initio approach. First, nuclear shell model calculations using established interactions (e.g., in the 50-82 proton shell) will be performed to compute the nuclear wavefunctions, electric field gradients, and magnetic dipole distributions, moving beyond the single-particle approximation. Second, these refined nuclear moments will be used as input for state-of-the-art relativistic coupled-cluster (RCC) calculations—including triple excitations—on selected atomic (e.g., Sn I, Bi I) and molecular (e.g., BiF, SbN) systems to compute electronic structure factors. The consistency between calculated and experimental HFS constants across multiple isotopes will be the key test.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles framework for decoupling nuclear structure effects from electronic correlation in hyperfine analysis. It will deliver a new set of benchmark nuclear quadrupole moments for key heavy elements like Sb and Bi, with rigorously quantified uncertainties. The results will directly impact the interpretation of precision spectroscopic data in fundamental symmetry tests and astrophysics.
proposals.novelty: While Pyykkö's group has pioneered the extraction of nuclear moments using molecular data, the explicit two-way feedback between detailed nuclear shell model wavefunctions and high-level relativistic electronic structure theory for explaining hyperfine anomalies represents a novel, integrated methodology not yet applied to heavy elements.

proposals.title: Testing the Atomic-State Independence of Reference Magnetic Dipole Moments for Precise Nuclear Moment Determination
proposals.motivation: The accurate extraction of nuclear magnetic dipole and electric quadrupole moments from hyperfine structure (HFS) constants relies on the assumption that the electronic factors (e.g., A/B ratios) are independent of the chosen reference isotope's nuclear moment. This assumption, while widely used, has not been systematically validated across different atomic states and heavy elements, potentially introducing unquantified errors into fundamental nuclear parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: The reference magnetic dipole moment used to calibrate electronic factors for extracting nuclear moments is not universally independent of atomic state; significant state-dependent deviations exist, particularly for heavy elements and open-shell configurations, challenging a key assumption in hyperfine analysis.
proposals.method: We will perform high-precision ab initio relativistic coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) calculations for a curated set of atoms (e.g., Sn, Sb, Bi, Tl) across multiple electronic states, including ground and excited states with different term symbols (e.g., ²P, ²D, ³P). For each state, we will compute the magnetic dipole hyperfine constant (A) and its electronic factor. Using known nuclear magnetic moments (μ_I) of two different isotopes as independent references, we will test for consistency in the derived electronic factors. The analysis will be extended to the electric quadrupole interaction (B constant) where applicable.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative test of a foundational assumption in hyperfine structure analysis. It will establish guidelines for the reliable extraction of nuclear moments from molecular and atomic data, and may lead to revised values for poorly known moments. The results will directly impact the precision of nuclear structure studies and tests of fundamental symmetries.
proposals.novelty: While the method of extracting moments using reference isotopes is standard, a systematic investigation of its inherent assumption across multiple atomic states and heavy elements, combining Pyykkö's expertise in relativistic calculations and nuclear moment determination, is novel and non-trivial.

proposals.title: Ab Initio Investigation of the Hyperfine Anomaly in s1/2 Nuclear Ground States: Resolving Deviations from the Linear Bohr-Weisskopf Relationship
proposals.motivation: The hyperfine anomaly, or Bohr-Weisskopf effect, provides a crucial probe of nuclear magnetization distributions beyond the point-nucleus model. For nuclei with spin I > 1/2, a linear relationship between the anomaly and the nuclear quadrupole moment is often observed, but this relationship breaks down for s1/2 nuclear ground states (I=1/2). Understanding this deviation is essential for extracting accurate nuclear moments from atomic and molecular spectroscopic data and for testing nuclear structure models.
proposals.hypothesis: The deviation for s1/2 states arises from a combination of significant contributions from higher-order nuclear moments (e.g., the nuclear octupole moment) and a pronounced sensitivity to the detailed radial distribution of the nuclear magnetization, which is not captured by the simple linear model derived for deformed nuclei.
proposals.method: We will employ high-precision relativistic coupled-cluster (RCC) theory, including single, double, and perturbative triple excitations (CCSD(T)), to calculate electronic factors for a selected set of atoms and ions with s1/2 nuclear ground states (e.g., 119Sn, 207Pb, 199Hg). The electronic wavefunctions will be used to compute hyperfine structure constants (A and B) and the associated field gradients. The nuclear structure input will be modeled using finite nucleus models with magnetization densities from nuclear density functional theory (DFT) calculations for the relevant isotopes. The calculated anomalies will be compared against the best available experimental data to isolate the contributions beyond the linear model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, first-principles explanation for the anomalous hyperfine behavior in s1/2 nuclei, moving beyond empirical correlations. It will deliver refined electronic factors and more accurate nuclear magnetic dipole moments for these key isotopes, directly impacting the precision of hyperfine structure analyses in atomic physics and astrophysics.
proposals.novelty: While Pyykkö's group has extensively studied quadrupole moments and hyperfine constants, a targeted ab initio investigation focusing specifically on the failure of the linear anomaly model for s1/2 states, incorporating advanced nuclear magnetization models, has not been performed.

proposals.title: Systematic ab initio analysis of the hyperfine anomaly across ionization states: A probe for nuclear magnetization distributions
proposals.motivation: The hyperfine anomaly, or Bohr-Weisskopf effect, arises from the finite distribution of nuclear magnetization and is a critical, yet often neglected, correction in precise determinations of nuclear moments from atomic and molecular spectra. Pre-2026, most studies focus on neutral atoms, leaving a gap in understanding how this anomaly behaves systematically across different ionization states for the same nucleus, which probes different electronic densities at the nucleus.
proposals.hypothesis: The hyperfine anomaly is not a fixed nuclear constant for a given isotope pair but varies measurably with the electronic configuration and ionization state due to changes in the effective penetration of s- and p-electrons into the nuclear volume.
proposals.method: We will employ high-level relativistic coupled-cluster (RCC) methods, including singles, doubles, and perturbative triples [RCCSD(T)], implemented within the DIRAC code package, to calculate electronic factors for hyperfine structure constants (A and B) for selected heavy elements (e.g., Sb, Bi, Pb). Calculations will be performed for neutral atoms and their corresponding ions (e.g., Sn I, Sn II; Bi I, Bi II) using finite nuclear models. The anomaly for isotope pairs will be extracted by comparing calculated and experimental hyperfine constants, following Pyykkö's established methodology for nuclear quadrupole moment determination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic dataset and theoretical analysis of ionization-state-dependent hyperfine anomalies, refining the extraction of nuclear moments from ionic spectra. It will offer new constraints on nuclear magnetization models and improve the accuracy of fundamental atomic properties used in tests of physics beyond the Standard Model.
proposals.novelty: While hyperfine anomalies are known, their explicit variation with ionization state has not been systematically quantified or exploited as a detailed probe of nuclear structure, moving beyond the typical treatment of the anomaly as a simple correction factor for neutral systems.

proposals.title: Ab Initio Nuclear DFT for Nuclear Quadrupole Moments: A Unified Approach from Nuclear Structure to Atomic Hyperfine Constants
proposals.motivation: The accurate determination of nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) remains a critical bottleneck in hyperfine structure analysis, as they are typically extracted by combining experimental molecular field gradients with calculated electronic factors, introducing significant uncertainty. A first-principles prediction of Q from nuclear structure alone would provide an independent benchmark and resolve long-standing discrepancies in hyperfine studies of heavy elements like Sb and Bi, which are central to Pyykkö's recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modern ab initio nuclear density functional theory (DFT), using state-of-the-art energy density functionals and accounting for quadrupole correlations, can predict nuclear quadrupole moments for a range of isotopes with sufficient accuracy to critically assess and replace empirically fitted values used in atomic and molecular physics.
proposals.method: We will employ the symmetry-unrestricted nuclear DFT framework, specifically using the Skyrme energy density functional (e.g., UNEDF1 or SV-min) to solve the Hartree-Fock-Bogoliubov equations for nuclear ground-state deformations. The nuclear charge distribution will be computed directly from the proton wavefunctions. The intrinsic spectroscopic quadrupole moment will be derived and converted to the laboratory-frame electric quadrupole moment Q using the rotational model for deformed nuclei. Calculations will be performed for chains of isotopes (e.g., Sn, Sb, Bi) using the HFBTHO or MOCCa codes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first table of ab initio nuclear quadrupole moments for key isotopes, enabling a direct, model-independent comparison with values 'back-calculated' from molecular data. It will establish a new protocol for hyperfine analysis where nuclear structure theory informs atomic physics, reducing the number of fitted parameters and improving the reliability of electronic factor extraction.
proposals.novelty: While nuclear DFT is used for nuclear properties and atomic physics calculates field gradients, the direct application of ab initio nuclear DFT to predict Q for the specific purpose of atomic hyperfine analysis is a novel, cross-disciplinary synthesis. It moves beyond using nuclear theory merely for systematics to providing quantitative, predictive input for resolving atomic-scale puzzles.

proposals.title: Calibrating Nuclear Quadrupole Moments via Muonic Atom Spectroscopy: A High-Precision Route for Heavy Elements
proposals.motivation: The accurate determination of nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) for heavy elements like Sb and Bi is hindered by the reliance on calculated electric field gradients (EFGs) from molecular or atomic data, introducing significant theoretical uncertainty. Independent, high-precision experimental benchmarks for these EFGs are scarce, limiting the reliability of extracted Q values. Muonic atom spectroscopy, where a muon replaces an electron, provides a direct, isotope-specific probe of the nuclear charge distribution and is exceptionally sensitive to nuclear quadrupole effects.
proposals.hypothesis: The hyperfine structure splittings in muonic atoms, measured via high-resolution X-ray spectroscopy, can be used to derive model-independent electric field gradients at the nucleus, thereby providing a primary calibration for the EFGs needed to extract nuclear quadrupole moments from conventional atomic/molecular data.
proposals.method: We will perform state-of-the-art relativistic coupled cluster (RCC) calculations, including Breit and QED corrections, to compute the electronic structure factors for target heavy elements (e.g., Sn, Sb, Bi). These will be combined with precisely measured quadrupole hyperfine splittings from muonic atom experiments (e.g., from PSI) to solve for the nuclear quadrupole moment Q. The obtained Q values will then be used to back-calculate and critically assess the EFGs from molecular systems studied in our previous work, establishing a calibrated benchmark set.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a set of benchmark nuclear quadrupole moments for key p-block elements with an order-of-magnitude improvement in accuracy. It will create a calibrated reference for future computational chemistry and nuclear physics studies, directly impacting the interpretation of hyperfine interactions across atomic, molecular, and solid-state systems.
proposals.novelty: While muonic atom data exists, its systematic use as a calibration tool for molecular EFG calculations in heavy-element quadrupole moment determination is a novel, cross-disciplinary approach that bypasses traditional theoretical error chains.

proposals.title: Ab initio determination of hyperfine structure constants and nuclear quadrupole moments for superheavy element 112 (copernicium) and its lighter homologues
proposals.motivation: The hyperfine structure of superheavy elements (SHEs) remains largely unexplored experimentally and theoretically, yet it provides a unique probe of relativistic and quantum electrodynamic (QED) effects on atomic electron densities near high-Z nuclei. For copernicium (Cn, Z=112), the closed-shell 7s² ground state suggests a simple electronic structure, but its hyperfine anomalies relative to cadmium and mercury could reveal profound relativistic contraction and nuclear volume effects, crucial for testing atomic theory at the limit of the periodic table.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hyperfine A and B constants for Cn will exhibit extreme deviations from non-relativistic scaling due to strong relativistic stabilization of the 7s and 7p orbitals, and that these deviations can be used to extract an improved, consistent nuclear quadrupole moment for the only known isotope, ²⁸⁵Cn.
proposals.method: We will employ high-level relativistic coupled-cluster (RCCSD(T)) and multiconfiguration Dirac-Hartree-Fock (MCDHF) methods, as implemented in codes like DIRAC and GRASP, to calculate electronic wavefunctions and hyperfine interaction factors for Cn and its homologues (Cd, Hg). Nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) will be determined by fitting calculated electric field gradients to available or estimated molecular data for Cn-containing compounds (e.g., CnH), following Pyykkö's established methodology. The calculations will systematically include Breit and QED corrections.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-accuracy theoretical benchmarks for the hyperfine structure of copernicium, offering predictions for future laser spectroscopy experiments. It will deliver a refined nuclear quadrupole moment for ²⁸⁵Cn, contributing to nuclear structure knowledge of SHEs, and quantify the relativistic and QED contributions to hyperfine interactions in the 7th period.
proposals.novelty: While Pyykkö's group has determined Q for lighter elements from molecular data, applying this precise methodology to a superheavy element with a closed-shell ground state to disentangle extreme relativistic electronic effects from nuclear parameters is a novel and formidable challenge.

proposals.title: Determining nuclear quadrupole moments of Cd isotopes from hyperfine structure in few-electron highly charged ions: a stringent test of relativistic many-body theory
proposals.motivation: The precise determination of nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) from atomic and ionic hyperfine structure (hfs) is a long-standing challenge in physics, bridging nuclear and atomic structure. Highly charged ions (HCIs) with few electrons provide a clean, relativistic system where electron correlation effects can be calculated with high accuracy, offering a unique opportunity to extract nuclear moments and test many-body methods. Cadmium (Z=48) has multiple stable isotopes with significant quadrupole moments, but their values, especially for neutron-rich isotopes, remain uncertain from direct nuclear measurements.
proposals.hypothesis: High-precision measurements of the hyperfine B factors in few-electron Cd HCIs (e.g., Cd^49+ in a hydrogen-like or lithium-like configuration), combined with state-of-the-art relativistic coupled-cluster (RCC) calculations including Breit and QED corrections, will allow the extraction of nuclear quadrupole moments with an order-of-magnitude improved accuracy and provide a critical benchmark for electron correlation effects in medium-Z systems.
proposals.method: We will perform high-resolution laser spectroscopy on a beam of highly charged ^Cd ions produced and trapped in an electron beam ion trap (EBIT) or a Paul trap. The hyperfine splittings will be measured using collinear laser spectroscopy or quantum logic spectroscopy techniques for precision. The theoretical analysis will employ the relativistic coupled-cluster (RCC) method, implemented in codes like DIRAC, to compute the electric field gradient at the nucleus. The calculations will be systematically expanded to include triple excitations (RCCSDT), estimate the Breit interaction, and incorporate dominant QED corrections (self-energy, vacuum polarization) to reach sub-percent accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, highly accurate set of nuclear quadrupole moments for the Cd isotopic chain, resolving long-standing discrepancies. It will provide one of the most stringent tests of relativistic many-body theory for hyperfine structure in medium-Z ions, quantifying correlation effects in a regime between light atoms and very heavy ions. The results will also serve as a benchmark for refining methods used to extract nuclear moments from molecular data for heavier elements like Sb and Bi.
proposals.novelty: While hfs in HCIs has been studied, a dedicated campaign on Cd ions, targeting the interplay between precise extraction of nuclear moments and the validation of high-order correlation effects in a few-electron, medium-Z system, is novel. This approach inverts the typical problem: instead of using a known Q to test theory, it uses a highly advanced, benchmarkable theory to pin down poorly known nuclear properties.

proposals.title: Calibrating Relativistic Multi-Configuration Methods via a Systematic Inverse Analysis of Nuclear Quadrupole Moments
proposals.motivation: The accuracy of state-of-the-art atomic calculations, such as relativistic multi-configuration Dirac-Hartree-Fock (MCDHF) and coupled-cluster methods, is fundamentally limited by the precision of input nuclear parameters and the treatment of electron correlation. Nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) are often derived from hyperfine structure measurements using these same calculations, creating a circular dependency. This project inverts the paradigm to use independently, precisely measured nuclear moments as benchmarks to rigorously test and refine atomic wavefunctions.
proposals.hypothesis: Discrepancies between calculated and experimentally derived electric field gradients (EFGs) for a series of heavy elements, when using benchmark nuclear Q moments, will systematically reveal and allow for the correction of specific deficiencies in relativistic atomic wavefunction models, particularly in core-polarization and correlation effects from inner shells.
proposals.method: We will select a curated set of heavy atoms and ions (e.g., Sb, Bi, Ta, Re) where high-precision nuclear quadrupole moments have been determined independently via muonic spectroscopy or collinear laser spectroscopy. For these systems, we will perform systematic MCDHF calculations using the GRASP2K code, progressively increasing the complexity of the active electron space. The inverse problem will be solved by adjusting the theoretical EFG to match the experimental hyperfine constant A(B) using the benchmark Q, quantifying the 'error' in the ab initio EFG. Bayesian optimization will be employed to correlate these errors with specific configuration spaces, guiding targeted expansions of the computational model.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a set of calibrated, high-fidelity atomic wavefunctions for heavy elements, directly validated against nuclear data. It will establish a new methodology for using nuclear moments as precision tools for atomic theory, moving beyond their traditional role as fitted parameters. The refined computational models will improve the predictive power for hyperfine structures and related properties across the periodic table.
proposals.novelty: While nuclear moments are typically outputs of atomic calculations, this proposal systematically uses them as fixed inputs to perform an inverse calibration of the underlying electronic theory, a targeted approach not commonly applied to multi-configuration methods for heavy elements. The use of a Bayesian framework to link EFG discrepancies to specific electron correlation effects provides a novel, quantitative diagnostic tool.

proposals.title: Relativistic Electronic Structure Constraints on Nuclear Stability: Defining the Next Island for Superheavy Elements up to Z=130
proposals.motivation: The synthesis and identification of superheavy elements (SHEs) rely on theoretical predictions of nuclear stability islands, yet current models exhibit significant discrepancies. A more precise mapping of shell closures and magic numbers beyond oganesson (Z=118) is crucial for guiding future experiments at facilities like GSI and RIKEN. This requires a synthesis of nuclear structure with the relativistic electronic effects that dominate in these extreme-Z regimes.
proposals.hypothesis: The inclusion of precise, fully relativistic electron-nucleus interactions—specifically the electric field gradient (EFG) at the nucleus derived from atomic calculations—will provide critical constraints for nuclear models, refining predictions for the next proton magic numbers and associated neutron shells in the superheavy region.
proposals.method: We will employ high-precision relativistic coupled-cluster (RCC) and Dirac-Fock methods to calculate atomic electronic factors, particularly the electric field gradients, for candidate superheavy elements (Z=119-130). These EFG values, sensitive to nuclear deformation, will be used as observables to constrain macroscopic-microscopic nuclear models (e.g., Finite-Range Droplet Model) and self-consistent mean-field models (e.g., Skyrme-Hartree-Fock). The iterative procedure will adjust nuclear deformation parameters to achieve consistency between calculated atomic hyperfine structure and the nuclear quadrupole moments implied by the nuclear models for specific isotopic chains.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a refined map of potential stability islands, pinpointing the most promising neutron numbers for synthesis near Z=120 and Z=126. It will establish a novel, atomic-physics-informed protocol for validating nuclear structure predictions in regions where direct experimental data is absent. The results will directly inform target-projectile combinations for upcoming SHE discovery campaigns.
proposals.novelty: While nuclear and atomic calculations are typically performed independently, this proposal directly uses high-precision relativistic atomic structure calculations as an external constraint to refine nuclear models for superheavy elements, creating a feedback loop between the two domains. This integrated approach is novel for mapping stability in the uncharted region beyond oganesson.

proposals.title: Systematic Ab Initio Investigation of the Role of Formally Empty (n+1)s and (n+1)p Orbitals in Bonding Across the p-Block: From Hypervalency to Relativistic Effects
proposals.motivation: The concept of 'formally empty' orbitals, such as the 3d in Si or the 6p in Pb, is central to explaining hypervalency and unusual bonding in main-group compounds, yet a quantitative, periodic-table-wide understanding is lacking. Pre-2026, relativistic effects, known to contract s and expand p/d orbitals, are predicted to significantly alter the energy and spatial availability of these orbitals, but systematic computational studies correlating electronic structure, nuclear quadrupole moments (NQMs), and bonding metrics are absent.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the contribution of formally empty (n+1)s and (n+1)p orbitals to molecular bonding follows a non-monotonic trend down a group, driven by a competition between relativistic stabilization of s orbitals and destabilization of p orbitals, which can be quantified via their impact on electric field gradients (EFGs) and molecular properties.
proposals.method: We will perform systematic, high-level relativistic ab initio calculations (using methods like 4-component Dirac-Hartree-Fock and coupled-cluster theory) on a curated set of p-block molecules (e.g., EX_n, E=Si-Pb, X=H, F, Cl). For each system, we will decompose molecular EFGs at the nucleus to extract the contribution of specific atomic orbital populations, leveraging Pyykkö's expertise in relating EFGs to NQMs. Natural Bond Orbital (NBO) and Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM) analyses will quantify the electron density in formally empty orbitals. The computational approach will be validated against precise hyperfine structure data from atomic spectroscopy where available.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, relativistic map of 'orbital availability' across the p-block, directly linking atomic electronic structure to molecular bonding motifs. It will refine the understanding of hypervalency and offer a new interpretation of NQMs in heavy elements as probes of bonding character, moving beyond the point-charge model.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, systematic coupling of nuclear quadrupole coupling analysis—a core strength of Pyykkö's group—with a quantum-chemical dissection of bonding into contributions from orbitals traditionally considered unoccupied, explicitly tracing the influence of relativistic effects on this partitioning.

proposals.title: Relativistic and Orbital-Crossing Origins of Secondary Periodicity: A Quantitative Ab Initio Analysis Across the 4p, 5p, and 6p Blocks
proposals.motivation: Secondary Periodicity—the non-monotonic variation of properties like atomic radii and ionization energies within p-blocks—is a well-established but incompletely explained phenomenon in the periodic table. Pre-2026 qualitative explanations invoke relativistic effects and the crossing of ns and (n-1)d orbitals, but a unified, quantitative quantum mechanical model is lacking. A rigorous explanation is crucial for deepening our understanding of periodic trends and for accurately predicting the chemistry of heavier elements.
proposals.hypothesis: The Secondary Periodicity observed for 4p, 5p, and 6p elements can be quantitatively reproduced and explained by the interplay of scalar relativistic effects, spin-orbit coupling, and the specific energy ordering and radial characteristics of valence ns, np, and (n-1)d orbitals, as captured by high-level relativistic ab initio calculations.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step computational approach. First, we will perform systematic, state-of-the-art relativistic coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) and multiconfigurational (CASSCF/MRCI) calculations on neutral atoms and key ions (e.g., M+, M-) across the p-block. Second, we will analyze the results using Pyykkö's established methods for decomposing effects: calculating expectation values of r^k operators for relevant orbital sets, evaluating relativistic contributions via Dirac-Coulomb versus non-relativistic Hamiltonians, and correlating these with observed periodic properties (e.g., radii, spectroscopic constants from molecular data).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fully quantitative, ab initio model linking specific relativistic and orbital mechanisms to the empirical Secondary Periodicity patterns. It will deliver a predictive framework for properties of heavy p-block elements and offer deeper insight into the 'uniqueness of 2p elements' by contrasting them with heavier congeners.
proposals.novelty: While the phenomenon is known, a complete decomposition and quantification of the competing relativistic, correlation, and orbital effects driving Secondary Periodicity, using methodologies refined for nuclear quadrupole moment analysis, has not been achieved.

proposals.title: Nuclear Quadrupole Moments and Electronic Structure in Extreme Potentials: Bridging Neutron Star Crust Physics and Atomic Theory
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the determination of nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) relies heavily on molecular and atomic hyperfine structure data, but these methods are calibrated for terrestrial, isolated nuclei. Simultaneously, astrophysical models of neutron star crusts describe matter at densities exceeding 10^11 g/cm^3, where nuclei are embedded in a degenerate electron gas, creating extreme, spatially varying electrostatic potentials. This presents a unique, untested environment to probe how such potentials influence the electronic factors governing hyperfine interactions, a regime inaccessible in laboratory atoms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the electron density and relativistic effects at nuclear sites in dense astrophysical matter significantly modify the electric field gradients (EFGs) used to extract nuclear quadrupole moments, implying that Q values derived from terrestrial data may require environment-dependent corrections under such extremes.
proposals.method: We will employ relativistic density functional theory (DFT) within the local density approximation, as used in neutron star crust models (e.g., the compressible liquid drop model in a Wigner-Seitz cell), to calculate the electron density and electrostatic potential. For selected heavy nuclei (e.g., Sb, Bi, Sn isotopes), we will compute the resulting EFG at the nucleus using established ab initio atomic-structure methods (e.g., Dirac-Hartree-Fock or coupled-cluster) but with boundary conditions and potentials informed by the astrophysical crust model. The sensitivity of the electronic factor f=EFG/Q to density and lattice structure will be systematically mapped.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative estimates of environment-dependent corrections to nuclear quadrupole moment determinations, establishing a novel link between nuclear structure physics and astrophysical condensed matter. It will yield new, theoretically informed constraints on Q values for neutron-rich isotopes relevant to nucleosynthesis and offer insights into electron behavior in ultra-strong, non-Coulombic central fields.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the well-developed formalism for dense astrophysical matter—a domain of nuclear and condensed matter physics—to a core problem of atomic and molecular quantum chemistry (determining nuclear moments), creating a new interdisciplinary benchmark for properties of atoms in extreme conditions.

proposals.title: Inverse Nuclear Design: Determining Nuclear Quadrupole Moments and Charge Radii to Target Specific Electronic Structures in Superheavy Elements
proposals.motivation: The synthesis and study of superheavy elements (Z > 103) is a frontier of nuclear and atomic physics. Current efforts focus on predicting electron shell stability from given nuclear properties. Inverting this problem—designing nuclei to achieve desired, potentially exotic, electron configurations—could guide experimental searches for new elements and isomers by identifying the most promising nuclear characteristics. This bridges Pyykkö's expertise in nuclear quadrupole moments and relativistic electronic structure calculations with the quest to understand the 'island of stability'.
proposals.hypothesis: For a target superheavy electron configuration (e.g., a specific ground state or a long-lived excited state), there exists a set of nuclear parameters—specifically, the electric quadrupole moment Q and the nuclear charge radius—that minimizes the total relativistic energy and maximizes its stability against autoionization or decay.
proposals.method: The research will employ a two-step, iterative computational framework. First, high-accuracy relativistic coupled-cluster (RCC) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, using codes like DIRAC, will map the electronic energy landscape as a function of nuclear Q and charge radius for candidate elements (e.g., Z=120, 122). Second, a Bayesian optimization algorithm will be used to solve the inverse problem, identifying the nuclear parameter sets that lead to the target electronic energy minima. The sensitivity of electron shells (e.g., 7p, 6d, 8s) to these nuclear moments will be quantified using electronic factor calculations analogous to Pyykkö's work on Sn I.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel mapping between nuclear structure targets and desired electronic properties, providing a new rationale for nuclear synthesis campaigns. It will quantify how 'fine-tuning' nuclear deformations could influence chemical properties in the superheavy region, offering a fresh perspective on the uniqueness of heavy elements. The methodology establishes a formal inverse-design paradigm for nuclear-electronic systems.
proposals.novelty: While forward calculations of electronic structure from nuclear data are standard, the explicit inverse problem—designing nuclei for electron shells—is largely unexplored. This approach treats nuclear parameters as design variables for atomic stability, a significant conceptual shift from traditional nuclear physics.

proposals.title: Temperature Dependence of Hyperfine Coupling Constants in Heavy-Element Systems: A Challenge to the Static Model in χ_spin Analysis
proposals.motivation: The analysis of temperature-dependent magnetic susceptibility (χ_spin) in paramagnetic systems containing heavy elements (e.g., Sb, Bi, Sn) routinely assumes hyperfine coupling constants (A) are temperature-independent. This assumption, while simplifying, is physically questionable as thermal expansion, lattice vibrations, and population of low-lying electronic states can modify electron densities at the nucleus. A rigorous test is needed to quantify potential errors in extracted nuclear quadrupole moments and electronic structure parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hyperfine coupling constants, particularly the electric field gradient (efg) and magnetic hyperfine (A) constants, exhibit a measurable and chemically significant temperature dependence in heavy p-block element compounds, leading to systematic errors in nuclear quadrupole moments derived from high-temperature molecular or solid-state data.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged computational approach using established pre-2026 methods. First, high-level relativistic coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations will be performed on benchmark diatomic molecules (e.g., SbF, BiF, SnO) to compute A and efg constants as a function of bond length, simulating thermal expansion. Second, ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations at relevant temperatures will be conducted to sample the vibrational configuration space, from which temperature-averaged hyperfine constants will be obtained. The results will be compared against experimental data where temperature-dependent hyperfine structure is available.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, ab initio quantification of temperature effects on hyperfine parameters for heavy elements. It will deliver corrected, temperature-aware protocols for extracting nuclear quadrupole moments from molecular data and improve the accuracy of electronic structure information derived from χ_spin measurements in coordination chemistry and solid-state physics.
proposals.novelty: While temperature effects on structure are well-known, their direct and quantitative impact on the fundamental hyperfine constants used in nuclear moment determination has not been rigorously challenged or modeled for heavy elements, representing a significant oversight in the field.

proposals.title: Revisiting Covalent Radii: A High-Precision Ab Initio Study of Anomalous Bond Lengths in H2 and F2
proposals.motivation: Covalent radii are fundamental for predicting molecular geometries, yet the H2 and F2 dimers are persistent outliers. The H-H bond is significantly shorter, and the F-F bond longer, than predicted by standard radius sums. This discrepancy challenges the robustness of the covalent radius model for the first- and second-row elements, limiting its predictive power. A precise, quantum-chemical explanation is needed to refine these foundational parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: The anomalous bond lengths in H2 and F2 arise from unique electronic structure effects—specifically, the absence of core electrons in H and significant inner-shell contraction and electron correlation in F—that are not captured by simple, element-averaged covalent radii. These effects can be quantified and incorporated into a refined model.
proposals.method: We will perform high-level relativistic coupled-cluster calculations (CCSD(T)) with quadruple-zeta basis sets (e.g., aug-cc-pVQZ) for H2, F2, and a series of diatomic hydrides and fluorides (e.g., HF, CH4, CF4). Bond lengths will be computed with extreme precision. The analysis will decompose the deviations into contributions from electron correlation (via MP2 vs. CCSD(T) comparison) and relativistic effects using the 4-component Dirac-Coulomb Hamiltonian. We will then derive element-specific correction factors based on the electron density distribution and effective nuclear charge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, quantitative correction to the covalent radii of hydrogen and fluorine, resolving long-standing anomalies. It will deliver a more accurate predictive model for bond lengths in main-group chemistry. The findings will also offer deeper insight into the limits of the spherical-atom approximation in covalent bonding.
proposals.novelty: While the anomalies are known, this study uniquely combines Pyykkö's expertise in relativistic atomic physics and precise molecular calculations to systematically deconvolve the physical origins (correlation, relativity, lack of core) and propose a physics-based correction, moving beyond empirical fitting.

proposals.title: Covalent Radii for High Oxidation States: A Systematic Ab Initio Study of p-Block Elements
proposals.motivation: The widely used covalent radii by Pyykkö and co-workers are defined for standard valencies, but many elements, especially in the p-block, exhibit higher oxidation states in compounds and clusters. The lack of reliable radii for these states hinders accurate molecular and solid-state structure prediction, as well as the interpretation of bonding in high-valency main-group chemistry, an area of growing synthetic interest pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that covalent radii for elements in high oxidation states (e.g., Cl(VII), S(VI), I(VII)) can be systematically derived from quantum chemical calculations and that these radii will show a consistent contraction relative to their standard-valency counterparts due to increased effective nuclear charge.
proposals.method: We will perform high-level relativistic ab initio calculations (using methods like CCSD(T) or MRCI) on a curated set of molecular species where the central atom is in a high oxidation state. For each target element and oxidation state, we will calculate equilibrium bond lengths to various ligands (e.g., O, F). The covalent radius for the central atom will be obtained by subtracting the known, well-defined ligand radius. The procedure will be automated using in-house scripts to analyze computational output, ensuring statistical robustness across multiple molecular references.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive set of covalent radii for high oxidation states, directly extending the established Pyykkö covalent radius system. It will enable more accurate modeling of bond lengths in inorganic and materials chemistry and offer quantitative insight into the bond contraction associated with high formal charge.
proposals.novelty: While atomic radii trends are known qualitatively, a systematic, quantum-chemically derived parameter set for high oxidation states, integrated into the established covalent radius framework, is novel. This moves beyond the typical group-valency assumption to address real, high-oxidation-state chemistry.

proposals.title: Core Electron Correlation Effects on Relativistic Bonding Descriptions in Heavy-Element Systems
proposals.motivation: The frozen core approximation is standard in relativistic quantum chemistry to reduce computational cost, but its validity for describing subtle bonding effects in heavy elements is not fully quantified. Pre-2026 work by Pyykkö and others has shown that relativistic effects fundamentally alter bonding, particularly in 6p and 7p block elements. However, the potential role of core polarization and core-valence correlation in modifying these relativistic descriptions remains an open question, directly impacting the accuracy of calculated properties like nuclear quadrupole moments and hyperfine constants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that including dynamic correlation from core electrons (specifically (n-1)d and (n-2)f shells for heavy atoms) will non-negligibly alter electron density distributions in bonding regions, leading to significant corrections to relativistic bonding descriptions and derived properties like field gradients.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step computational protocol using established pre-2026 methods. First, high-accuracy relativistic coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) and multiconfigurational (CASSCF/MRCI) calculations will be performed on benchmark diatomic systems (e.g., BiH, SbN) using Dyall's core-valence basis sets. The core correlation will be included by explicitly correlating electrons from the outermost core shells (e.g., 5s5p5d for Bi). The effects will be quantified by comparing electron densities, bond lengths, vibrational frequencies, and electric field gradients at the nucleus against frozen-core results. The DIRAC and BAGEL program packages will be used for these calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic assessment of core correlation's importance in relativistic bonding models, offering concrete correction factors for future high-precision studies. It will deliver revised, more accurate electronic factors for hyperfine structure and improved benchmarks for determining nuclear quadrupole moments from molecular data, enhancing the interplay between atomic physics and quantum chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While core correlation is studied in non-relativistic contexts, its targeted investigation within a fully relativistic framework to test the robustness of established relativistic bonding mechanisms—like the 6p element 'relativistic stabilisation of s-orbitals'—is novel. The focus on its impact on properties sensitive to electron density near the nucleus (field gradients) is a specific and underexplored angle.

proposals.title: Relativistic Decomposition of Interatomic Interactions: Scalar and Spin-Orbit Contributions to Electrostatic and Orbital Terms in Heavy-Element Systems
proposals.motivation: The profound influence of relativistic effects on the chemistry of heavy elements is well-established, but a quantitative, energy-decomposed understanding of how scalar relativistic (SR) and spin-orbit (SO) coupling separately modulate fundamental bonding components is lacking. This gap limits the predictive design of materials and catalysts containing 5p and 6p elements, where both effects are significant. An analysis within the framework of energy decomposition analysis (EDA) would provide unprecedented mechanistic insight.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scalar relativistic effects predominantly stabilize electrostatic (classical) interactions through orbital contraction, while spin-orbit coupling selectively and significantly modifies orbital interaction (covalent/charge transfer) energies, with the magnitude of this modulation being system- and oxidation-state dependent.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step relativistic EDA protocol using the ADF program suite. First, for selected diatomic or small cluster systems containing Sb, Bi, Sn, or Pb, we will perform EDA at the four-component Dirac–Hartree–Fock (DHF) level as the reference. Second, we will decompose this result by performing parallel EDA calculations using the Zeroth-Order Regular Approximation (ZORA) with SR-only and SR+SO Hamiltonians, and by utilizing a scalar-relativistic pseudopotential (ECP) basis set for the heavy atom in non-relativistic calculations. The difference between SR-ZORA and ECP results quantifies the SR effect on each EDA term; the difference between DHF/SR+SO-ZORA and SR-ZORA results quantifies the SO effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative map separating scalar and spin-orbit relativistic contributions to electrostatic, Pauli repulsion, and orbital interaction energies. It will directly connect Pyykkö's expertise in nuclear quadrupole moments and hyperfine structure—properties exquisitely sensitive to relativistic electron densities—to the broader language of chemical bonding analysis. The results will offer design principles for tuning specific interaction components in heavy-element chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While relativistic EDA has been performed, a systematic isolation and comparison of scalar versus spin-orbit effects on individual bonding energy components, validated against four-component benchmarks, represents a novel and non-trivial advance beyond standard relativistic trend analyses.

proposals.title: Relativistic Bonding in Heavy p-Block Ions: A QTAIM Analysis of Electron Density Topology
proposals.motivation: The unique chemistry of heavy elements, particularly the 6p-block, is driven by strong relativistic effects that contract s- and d-orbitals while expanding p-orbitals, profoundly influencing bonding.
