Pre-2026, Pekka Pyykkö's work has elucidated these effects via hyperfine constants and nuclear quadrupole moments, but a systematic topological analysis of the resulting electron density in molecular ions is lacking. Applying the Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM) offers a rigorous, real-space framework to quantify these relativistic bonding changes directly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that relativistic effects in heavy p-block ions (e.g., Tl+, Pb2+, Bi3+, and their molecular complexes) will manifest as significant, quantifiable changes in QTAIM bond critical point (BCP) properties—such as electron density, Laplacian, and ellipticity—compared to non-relativistic calculations, revealing distinct bonding character not seen in lighter congeners.
proposals.method: We will perform relativistic two-component Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using the Dirac-Coulomb Hamiltonian and established functionals (e.g., PBE0) for a series of molecular ions containing heavy p-block elements. The all-electron electron densities will be analyzed using the QTAIM methodology as implemented in the AIMAll package. Specific properties at bond critical points (ρ(r), ∇²ρ(r), ε) and atomic basin charges will be computed and compared directly with non-relativistic (scalar) calculations to isolate relativistic contributions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive QTAIM-based topological fingerprint of relativistic bonding in heavy ions, directly linking Pyykkö's established work on nuclear quadrupole moments and hyperfine structure to concrete changes in chemical bonding descriptors. It will establish quantitative benchmarks for interpreting spectroscopic and molecular data of heavy-element compounds through the lens of electron density topology.
proposals.novelty: While QTAIM is a standard tool, its systematic application to isolate and quantify relativistic effects on bond topology in heavy p-block ions is novel, merging Pyykkö's expertise in relativistic atomic physics with the chemically intuitive BCP analysis to explain the 'uniqueness' of these elements from a density perspective.

proposals.title: Validation of Relativistic Electronic Structure Calculations for Heavy p-Block Elements via Core-Level X-ray Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: High-precision ab initio calculations for heavy elements, a hallmark of Pyykkö's work, are critical for interpreting hyperfine structure and nuclear quadrupole moments. However, the accuracy of the underlying electronic wavefunctions, especially for core and semi-core shells influenced by relativistic and electron correlation effects, is often inferred indirectly. Direct experimental validation of these calculated electronic structures is needed to confirm their reliability for nuclear property determination.
proposals.hypothesis: That state-of-the-art relativistic coupled cluster (CCSD(T)) or multi-configuration Dirac-Hartree-Fock (MCDHF) calculations of core-electron binding energies and orbital character for heavy p-block elements (e.g., Sn, Sb, Bi, Pb) will show quantitative agreement with high-resolution X-ray Absorption Spectroscopy (XAS) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements.
proposals.method: We will perform systematic computational studies on selected heavy element systems (atoms, small molecules like BiH3 or SnO) using the DIRAC code for relativistic 4-component calculations and the FLEUR code for solid-state systems. Core-level spectra will be simulated using the ΔSCF method for binding energies (XPS) and the Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE) within the OCEAN code for near-edge structure (XANES). These theoretical spectra will be directly compared to new, high-resolution synchrotron-based XAS and XPS data obtained from partners at MAX IV or ESRF.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, element-specific benchmark for advanced electronic structure methods in the heavy-element regime. It will strengthen the foundation for using such calculations to extract precise nuclear quadrupole moments from molecular data. The validated computational protocols can then be reliably applied to systems where direct experimental nuclear data is unavailable.
proposals.novelty: While Pyykkö's group has extensively calculated hyperfine parameters, direct validation against core-level spectroscopy—a sensitive probe of relativistic and correlation effects precisely where nuclear moments interact—is not standard practice. This proposal creates a novel feedback loop where nuclear property research validates its own electronic-structure foundation via a different experimental observable.

proposals.title: Relativistic and Electron Correlation Effects on Electric Field Gradients in 5d (TaF_x) and 4f (PrF_x) Fluorides: A Comparative Ab Initio Study for Nuclear Quadrupole Moment Determination
proposals.motivation: Accurate nuclear quadrupole moments (NQMs) for 5d and 4f elements are crucial for interpreting hyperfine structure data but remain poorly determined. Pre-2026, Pyykkö's group has successfully extracted NQMs for heavy p-block elements (e.g., Bi, Sb) from molecular electric field gradients (EFGs). Extending this methodology to earlier transition metals and lanthanides presents a significant challenge due to the complex interplay of relativistic, electron correlation, and open-shell effects in these systems, which is not yet fully quantified.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, high-level relativistic coupled-cluster study of small fluoride molecules (e.g., TaF_5, PrF_3) will allow for the accurate decomposition of relativistic contributions to the EFG, enabling the extraction of refined NQMs for ^181Ta and ^141Pr, and revealing distinct trends in 5d versus 4f element bonding.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-step computational protocol. First, molecular structures of selected Ta and Pr fluorides will be optimized using relativistic density functional theory (DFT) with the ZORA Hamiltonian. Subsequently, benchmark calculations of the EFG at the nucleus will be performed using relativistic coupled-cluster methods (CCSD(T)) with all-electron basis sets (e.g., dyall.aeXz), systematically increasing the correlation treatment and basis set size. The direct relativistic (Dirac) and scalar relativistic contributions will be separated using the DKH2 Hamiltonian. The calculated EFGs will be combined with available experimental nuclear quadrupole coupling constants to derive NQMs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide critically evaluated NQMs for key ^181Ta and ^141Pr isotopes, filling a gap in nuclear data. It will deliver a quantitative analysis of how relativity and correlation compete in shaping the EFG for 5d versus 4f elements, offering new benchmarks for theoretical methods. The results will enhance the interpretation of spectroscopic data in astrophysics and materials science involving these elements.
proposals.novelty: While Pyykkö's recent work focused on heavy p-block elements, a systematic, high-accuracy ab initio comparison of EFGs in isovalent fluorides of a 5d transition metal and a 4f lanthanide, targeting NQM refinement, has not been conducted. The study directly tests the limits of established computational protocols in regimes of strong relativistic and open-shell correlation effects.

proposals.title: Relativistic Quantum Chemical Prediction of Structure and Bonding in Hypothetical Octafluorido Complexes of Seaborgium and Flerovium: [SgF8]2- and [FlF8]2-
proposals.motivation: The chemistry of superheavy elements (SHEs) is a frontier for testing the limits of the periodic table and relativistic quantum theory. Pre-2026, experimental studies on SHEs like Sg (element 106) and Fl (element 114) are extremely challenging, limited to single-atom, gas-phase experiments. Computational predictions of their potential molecular compounds, especially high-coordination complexes, are crucial for guiding future experiments and understanding the role of relativistic effects, including spin-orbit coupling and the scalar-relativistic contraction/expansion of orbitals, on chemical bonding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that due to extreme relativistic effects, particularly the strong spin-orbit coupling and the relativistic stabilization of the 6d orbitals in Sg and the destabilization of the 7p1/2 orbital in Fl, the hypothetical [SgF8]2- ion will be a stable, high-symmetry (likely cubic Oh) complex, while [FlF8]2- will exhibit significant structural distortion or instability, challenging its existence as a classical coordination anion.
proposals.method: We will employ state-of-the-art relativistic two-component density functional theory (DFT) and wavefunction-based methods. Initial geometry optimizations and vibrational frequency analyses for [MF8]2- (M = Sg, Fl) and lighter group 6 and 14 homologs (e.g., [WF8]2-, [PbF8]2-) for benchmarking will be performed using the Dirac program package, utilizing the Zeroth-Order Regular Approximation (ZORA) or the exact two-component (X2C) Hamiltonian. Electron correlation will be treated with hybrid functionals (e.g., PBE0, B3LYP). Subsequent high-level coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) single-point energy calculations on optimized geometries will be conducted using relativistic effective core potentials (RECPs) for the heavy elements to refine bonding energies and electronic structures. Bonding will be analyzed via Natural Population Analysis (NPA), Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM), and orbital analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first detailed quantum chemical predictions for octafluorido complexes of Sg and Fl, quantifying the extent of covalent bonding and electrostatic stabilization. It will deliver benchmark-quality data on bond lengths, vibrational frequencies, and complex stability, offering concrete molecular targets for future experimental SHE chemistry. The study will deepen the understanding of how relativistic effects govern the coordination chemistry and maximal oxidation states in the 7th period.
proposals.novelty: While relativistic calculations on diatomic SHE compounds exist, a systematic study predicting the feasibility and properties of high-coordination, high-oxidation-state molecular anions like [SgF8]2- and [FlF8]2- is unprecedented. The direct comparison between a 6d-transition metal and a 7p-main-group element in identical ligand fields will uniquely highlight the divergent chemical consequences of relativity across the periodic table.

proposals.title: Relativistic Ligands as Drivers of Bonding: A Systematic ab initio Study of Heavy Halide (I, At) Effects on Molecular Electronic Structure and Nuclear Quadrupole Coupling
proposals.motivation: Relativistic quantum chemistry has predominantly focused on the effects of heavy central atoms (e.g., transition metals, lanthanides). However, in complexes with heavy ligands like iodide or astatine, the ligand's own strong relativistic effects—spin-orbit coupling, scalar relativistic contraction/expansion—can fundamentally alter bonding. This 'inverse problem' is poorly quantified but critical for interpreting spectroscopic data, including nuclear quadrupole coupling constants used to determine nuclear moments.
proposals.hypothesis: The relativistic character of a heavy ligand (e.g., I, At) is a primary, non-perturbative factor governing the electron density distribution in molecular bonds, directly measurable through its influence on electric field gradients (EFGs) at both the ligand and a lighter central atom.
proposals.method: We will perform systematic 4-component relativistic Dirac-Hartree-Fock and coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) calculations on series of diatomic and small polyatomic molecules (e.g., X–M, where X = Cl, Br, I, At and M = light p-block or d-block element). The electronic contributions to EFGs at all nuclei will be decomposed using Pyykkö's established methods. Calculations will isolate scalar and spin-orbit relativistic effects via sequential inclusion (DKS vs. ZORA). The molecular EFGs will be correlated with nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) from recent literature (e.g., for 127I, 121/123Sb, 209Bi) to validate the approach and refine Q values.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative framework for 'ligand-driven relativity' in bonding, providing benchmark data and correction factors for interpreting hyperfine structure. It will yield refined nuclear quadrupole moments for heavy halogens by using molecules where the ligand is the heaviest element, minimizing uncertainty from metal-centered relativity.
proposals.novelty: It systematically inverts the traditional relativistic chemistry paradigm by making the ligand's relativity the primary variable, a perspective largely unexplored in high-level ab initio studies pre-2026, despite its implications for the entire periodic table.

proposals.title: Relativistic Effects and Ionic Bonding in Tetrahedral MF4 (M = Pb, Sn, Ge): A Quantum Chemical Investigation of Coordination Number Influence
proposals.motivation: Pyykkö's seminal work on 'relativistic' ionic radii and bonding, particularly for heavy p-block elements, has been developed primarily for high-coordination-number solids like octahedral halides. The influence of coordination number, a key parameter in inorganic chemistry, on this relativistic picture remains unexplored. Studying tetrahedral MF4 species, accessible in the gas phase or matrix-isolated, provides a direct test of how relativistic contraction and related effects manifest in lower-symmetry, molecular environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reduction in coordination number from octahedral to tetrahedral will amplify the relative importance of scalar relativistic and spin-orbit coupling effects on metal-ligand bonding, leading to measurable changes in electron density distributions, bond lengths, and vibrational frequencies compared to non-relativistic predictions.
proposals.method: We will perform systematic, high-level ab initio calculations on tetrahedral MF4 (M = Ge, Sn, Pb) using coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) and relativistic density functional theory (DFT). The Douglas-Kroll-Hess or exact two-component (X2C) Hamiltonian will treat relativistic effects. We will compute equilibrium geometries, harmonic vibrational frequencies, and electron density descriptors (e.g., QTAIM, NBO). The electronic contributions to nuclear quadrupole coupling constants (NQCC) for 73Ge, 117/119Sn, and 209Bi (doped) will be calculated to probe electric field gradients, linking to Pyykkö's recent work on determining nuclear moments.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the understanding of relativistic ionic bonding beyond crystalline solids to molecular units, establishing a coordination-number-dependent framework. It will provide benchmark electronic structure data for heavy tetrahalides and clarify the interplay between coordination geometry, relativity, and covalency.
proposals.novelty: While relativistic effects in heavy-atom molecules are well-studied, a systematic investigation targeting the specific role of coordination number in modulating these effects for ionic bonding, using a combination of property calculations (including NQCC) directly tied to Pyykkö's recent methodological focus, is novel.

proposals.title: Beyond Gold: A Systematic Ab Initio Search for Relativistic Stabilization and Contraction in Late 5d and 6p Block Elements
proposals.motivation: The unique chemistry of gold, including its high electron affinity, relativistic contraction, and distinctive color, is often attributed to pronounced relativistic effects in its 5d and 6s shells. This has created a 'gold-uniqueness' paradigm in relativistic quantum chemistry. However, a systematic, high-accuracy computational survey of neighboring elements (e.g., Pt, Hg, Tl, Pb, Bi) to identify analogous or even more extreme relativistic signatures is lacking. Such a study is crucial for a unified understanding of periodic trends in the heaviest p- and d-block elements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that elements adjacent to gold in the 5d and 6p series possess similarly strong or even stronger relativistic effects in specific electronic states or molecular environments, and that these effects can be systematically quantified via hyperfine and electronic structure constants.
proposals.method: We will employ high-level relativistic ab initio methods, specifically 4-component Dirac-Coulomb(-Gaunt) coupled-cluster (DC-CCSD(T)) and multiconfigurational approaches (MRCI), as implemented in codes like DIRAC and BAGEL. Calculations will target key atomic properties (e.g., electron affinities, excitation energies, s- and p- orbital contraction) and molecular hyperfine structure constants (A and B factors) for selected diatomic molecules (e.g., hydrides and halides) of Pt, Au, Hg, Tl, Pb, and Bi. The analysis will decompose contributions from scalar relativity, spin-orbit coupling, and electron correlation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative map of relativistic effects across the late 5d/6p block, moving beyond single-element anecdotes. It will establish a new baseline for interpreting spectroscopic data and nuclear quadrupole moments in this region. The results may reveal new 'champion' elements for specific relativistic phenomena, refining our understanding of chemical periodicity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the established focus on gold's uniqueness to perform a systematic, comparative survey of its neighbors using state-of-the-art relativistic electronic structure methods, explicitly linking atomic effects to measurable molecular hyperfine parameters.

proposals.title: A Self-Consistent Effective Medium Theory for Covalent Bond Lengths: Moving Beyond Additive Radii
proposals.motivation: The empirical additivity of covalent radii, while useful, is a crude approximation that fails for polar bonds, systems with significant hybridization, or heavy elements where relativistic effects are important. A more fundamental, predictive model for equilibrium bond lengths is needed in computational chemistry and materials science, particularly for designing novel compounds and interpreting spectroscopic data. Pre-2026, the limitations of tabulated radii are well-known, but a systematic, physics-based framework to replace them is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: The equilibrium length of a covalent bond A-B can be modeled as emerging from a self-consistent embedding problem, where each atom is treated as an impurity in an effective medium defined by the perturbed electron density of its partner, rendering the simple sum of atomic radii invalid.
proposals.method: We will adapt the mathematical formalism of Effective Medium Theory (EMT) from materials physics to the quantum-chemical dimer problem. The method involves: (1) Defining a reference electron density for the isolated atoms using high-accuracy relativistic methods (e.g., Dirac-Hartree-Fock). (2) Formulating a self-consistent cycle where the effective potential for atom A is constructed from the density of atom B (and vice versa), incorporating charge transfer via a linear response function. (3) Solving for the equilibrium distance where the total energy of the coupled EMT system is minimized. The implementation will leverage Pyykkö's expertise in relativistic atomic calculations and nuclear quadrupole moment analysis to parameterize core polarization effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, non-empirical theoretical framework for predicting bond lengths, directly quantifying the non-additive contributions from charge transfer and polarization. It will yield a new set of 'effective' atomic radii that are context-dependent, offering superior accuracy for heavy-element and polar covalent bonds, with direct applications in molecular spectroscopy and the interpretation of hyperfine structure.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual cross-disciplinary transfer of the EMT framework from composite materials to the quantum mechanics of a diatomic bond, and in the explicit, self-consistent treatment of the bonded atoms as mutually perturbing effective media, a departure from perturbation theory or simple force-field approaches.

proposals.title: First-Principles Prediction of Interatomic Distances via Machine-Learned Atomic Electron Density Profiles
proposals.motivation: The concept of fixed atomic radii is a severe limitation in computational chemistry and materials science, failing for systems with unusual oxidation states, coordination, or relativistic effects. A more fundamental, transferable descriptor based on the atomic electron density is needed. This project aims to replace tabulated radii with a predictive model of environment-dependent atomic density, directly linking electronic structure to geometry.
proposals.hypothesis: The spherically averaged radial electron density profile of an atom in any chemical environment can be accurately learned by a neural network from quantum chemical data, and the equilibrium distance between two such atoms can be determined from a physically justified intersection criterion of their predicted density profiles.
proposals.method: We will train a deep neural network (e.g., a graph neural network or a transformer) on a large dataset of atomic density profiles extracted from DFT or coupled-cluster calculations of diverse molecules and solids. The input will be atomic number, oxidation state, coordination number, and neighbor identities. The output is the radial density distribution. Bond lengths will be computed by finding the distance where the interacting profiles satisfy a criterion like the minimum of the total density Laplacian, a well-established indicator of covalent bonding from Bader's Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational, first-principles-based tool for predicting geometries without empirical radii, applicable to novel compounds, high-pressure phases, and systems with strong relativistic effects. It will directly connect Pyykkö's expertise in relativistic effects and nuclear quadrupole moments (which probe the electron density) to a predictive model of structure.
proposals.novelty: The approach completely abandons the scalar radius paradigm, instead using a machine-learned, continuous density profile as the atomic representation. The direct prediction of geometry from the intersection of these ab initio-informed profiles, rather than from summed parameters, is a novel and physically grounded strategy.

proposals.title: Relativistic ab initio derivation of chemically-adaptive covalent radii for heavy and superheavy elements
proposals.motivation: The structural chemistry of heavy and superheavy elements (Z > 100) is poorly understood, with experimental data scarce. Existing covalent radius tables, largely based on lighter elements and fixed bond orders, fail to capture the extreme relativistic effects and variable bonding environments critical for these systems. A predictive, first-principles model is urgently needed to guide the synthesis and characterization of new compounds in the expanding superheavy region of the periodic table.
proposals.hypothesis: The covalent radius of a heavy element can be accurately modeled as a continuous, transferable function of its oxidation state, coordination number, and bond order, derived from high-level relativistic quantum chemical calculations on benchmark molecular systems.
proposals.method: We will employ 4-component relativistic coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) and density functional theory (DFT) with exact two-component (X2C) Hamiltonians to calculate equilibrium bond lengths for a curated set of molecular species containing heavy (e.g., Pb, Bi) and superheavy (e.g., Cn, Fl) elements across various oxidation states and coordination numbers. For each element, radii will be extracted via the difference method. A machine learning model (e.g., Gaussian Process Regression) will then be trained on this ab initio dataset to parameterize the radius as a function of the chemical descriptors, ensuring smooth interpolation and extrapolation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first unified, chemically-adaptive covalent radius database for elements up to Z ~ 120, directly useful for predicting molecular geometries in unexplored chemical space. It will provide fundamental insight into how relativistic and shell-structure effects modulate bonding across the periodic table, offering a critical tool for planning experiments in superheavy element chemistry.
proposals.novelty: The model moves beyond static, single-value radii by making them continuous functions of chemical context, and it is grounded entirely in first-principles data for superheavy elements, avoiding empirical extrapolation from lighter congeners.

proposals.title: Ab Initio Derivation of Oxidation-State- and Coordination-Dependent Covalent Radii for p-Block Elements
proposals.motivation: Covalent radii are fundamental parameters in chemistry and materials science, yet tabulated values are typically single, element-specific constants. This assumption fails for elements like Sb and Bi, where nuclear quadrupole coupling constants in molecules reveal significant electronic structure variations with oxidation state and coordination. A more accurate, context-dependent model is needed to improve predictive power in molecular structure, bonding analysis, and the interpretation of hyperfine interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: The covalent radius of an atom is not a fixed constant but a variable property that can be systematically predicted from first principles as a function of its formal oxidation state and coordination number, particularly for heavy p-block elements.
proposals.method: We will perform high-level relativistic coupled-cluster (CCSD(T)) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations on a curated set of molecular systems containing Sb, Bi, Sn, and other relevant p-block elements in various oxidation states and coordinations. For each system, we will compute electron density distributions and define an effective covalent radius from the position of the density minimum along the bond path (the bond critical point). A machine learning model (e.g., Gaussian Process Regression) will then be trained on this ab initio dataset to predict radii as a function of atomic number, oxidation state, coordination number, and key electronic descriptors like natural population analysis (NPA) charge.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, ab initio-based dataset and predictive model for context-dependent covalent radii. It will directly improve the accuracy of interpreting molecular spectroscopic data, especially nuclear quadrupole moments derived from hyperfine structure, and offer a refined tool for rationalizing chemical bonding trends across the periodic table.
proposals.novelty: While the variability of atomic size is acknowledged, this proposal systematically quantifies and predicts covalent radii from quantum mechanics for specific chemical environments, moving beyond empirical averages and directly linking the concept to computable electron density features.

proposals.title: Anisotropic Electron Density Profiles for Quadruple Bonds: Decomposing σ, π, and δ Components from First Principles
proposals.motivation: The concept of a spherical covalent radius is inadequate for describing the complex, anisotropic electron density in high-order metal-metal bonds, such as quadruple bonds in dimolybdenum or dirhenium complexes. This oversimplification hinders the predictive understanding of molecular geometries, ligand steric interactions, and bond strength trends crucial for inorganic synthesis and catalyst design. A quantitative, component-resolved map of bonding density is needed to move beyond empirical rules.
proposals.hypothesis: The total bond order density in metal-metal quadruple bonds can be computationally decomposed into distinct, spatially anisotropic σ, π, and δ profiles, and these profiles will be systematically parameterizable by element, oxidation state, and principal quantum number, revealing periodic trends obscured by spherical models.
proposals.method: We will employ high-level relativistic ab initio methods (e.g., CCSD(T) with all-electron basis sets) to compute the electron densities of prototypical M2L8 complexes (M = Cr, Mo, W, Re, Tc). The anisotropic bond density will be analyzed using the Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM) and the Electron Localization Function (ELF). The σ, π, and δ components will be isolated via a symmetry-adapted density decomposition protocol, fitting the resulting spatial profiles to parameterized analytical functions (e.g., modified Slater-type orbitals) for each component.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first database of anisotropic bond density profiles for quadruple bonds, providing a physically rigorous tool for predicting internuclear distances and ligand coordination geometries. It will establish quantitative links between the individual orbital contributions and observable properties like bond vibrational frequencies and nuclear quadrupole coupling constants, bridging electronic structure with spectroscopic data.
proposals.novelty: While bond orders are commonly discussed, a direct first-principles decomposition and spatial parameterization of the σ, π, and δ electron density components for quadruple bonds has not been achieved. This approach moves beyond integrated bond indices to provide a 3D, chemically intuitive map of bonding anisotropy.

proposals.title: Inverse Determination of Effective Bond Radii in Heavy-Element Clusters via Spectroscopic Tomography and Bayesian Inference
proposals.motivation: Covalent radii for heavy and superheavy elements, especially in unusual oxidation states or coordination environments, are poorly constrained and often derived from sparse structural data. Spectroscopic data is abundant but provides only indirect structural information. A robust, general method to extract structural parameters from spectra would enable the characterization of compounds where direct diffraction methods fail, such as short-lived species or complex solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: That a parameterized quantum chemical model, coupled with a geophysics-inspired inversion algorithm, can reliably extract a consistent set of effective bond radii for a metal center from its experimental electronic and vibrational spectra, treating the spectra as a composite signal of the underlying atomic geometry.
proposals.method: We will develop a forward model using relativistic density functional theory (DFT) to simulate UV-Vis and IR spectra for a target system (e.g., a bismuth or antimony halide cluster) as a function of a parameterized set of metal-ligand radii. The inverse problem will be solved using a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling algorithm within a Bayesian inference framework, analogous to seismic tomography inversions. The algorithm will iteratively adjust the radii in the forward model to minimize the misfit between calculated and observed spectra, yielding a probability distribution for the optimal radii.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational spectroscopy tool for structural elucidation where crystallography is not possible. It will deliver refined, spectroscopy-derived effective radii for heavy p-block elements in specific bonding environments, offering data complementary to traditional covalent radii tables. The methodology establishes a direct conceptual and technical bridge between geophysical inversion techniques and quantum chemical analysis.
proposals.novelty: The explicit adaptation of seismic tomography's model-based inversion philosophy to molecular spectroscopy for structural parameter extraction is unprecedented. While fitting geometries to spectra is common, framing it as a formal inverse problem with Bayesian uncertainty quantification for radii is a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Quantitative Decomposition of Relativistic Effects in Aurophilic Bonding: A Scalar and Spin-Orbit Partitioning Study
proposals.motivation: The closed-shell Au(I)...Au(I) aurophilic attraction is a celebrated example of a bond dominated by relativistic effects, but a quantitative, component-wise energetic decomposition is lacking. Current understanding relies on comparing fully relativistic and non-relativistic calculations, obscuring the distinct and potentially opposing roles of scalar relativistic (mass-velocity, Darwin) and spin-orbit coupling terms. A precise dissection is crucial for developing accurate, interpretable models for heavy-element chemistry and benchmarking new relativistic methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scalar relativistic effects are the primary drivers of aurophilic bond contraction and stabilization, while spin-orbit coupling provides a significant, structure-dependent correction that can be either bonding or anti-bonding. The net relativistic stabilization emerges from a delicate balance of these components.
proposals.method: We will perform high-level ab initio calculations (CCSD(T)) on benchmark [X-Au-PH3]2 dimers (X = Cl, CN, CH3) using the DIRAC program package. The relativistic Hamiltonian will be systematically deconstructed using the Exact-Two-Component (X2C) framework, enabling calculations with: 1) full relativity, 2) scalar relativity only (X2C with spin-orbit omitted), and 3) non-relativistic limit. Potential energy curves will be computed for each regime to extract binding energies (De) and equilibrium distances (Re). Electron density difference maps (e.g., Δρ = ρ(full rel) - ρ(scalar rel)) will visualize the spatial impact of spin-orbit coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first numerically ranked, component-resolved energy partitioning for aurophilic bonding, moving beyond qualitative statements. It will establish which relativistic terms are strictly necessary for a qualitative description and which fine-tune the interaction. The results will serve as a stringent benchmark for validating more approximate relativistic methods in density functional theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic adversarial scaling of relativistic operators within a single, consistent computational framework (X2C) to isolate their individual contributions, rather than the traditional binary comparison. This approach reveals the specific, and possibly competing, mechanistic roles of different relativistic effects.

proposals.title: Calibrating Periodic Models of Aurophilic Networks via High-Accuracy Cluster Benchmarks and Nuclear Quadrupole Couplings
proposals.motivation: Metallophilic interactions, particularly aurophilic bonds, are crucial in supramolecular chemistry and materials science, dictating the structure and properties of gold-based networks. However, standard Density Functional Theory (DFT) fails to describe these weak, closed-shell interactions quantitatively, leading to unreliable predictions for extended systems. A robust, multi-scale framework is needed to bridge the gap between accurate molecular quantum chemistry and predictive solid-state models.
proposals.hypothesis: The nuclear quadrupole coupling constants of heavy atoms (e.g., Au, Sb, Bi) in small aurophilic clusters, calculated via high-level ab initio methods, provide a sensitive electronic benchmark that can be used to calibrate a system-specific, dispersion-corrected DFT functional, enabling the accurate prediction of the electronic structure of extended metallophilic networks.
proposals.method: First, we will perform high-accuracy CCSD(T) and relativistic 4-component calculations on finite [Au(I)]_n and mixed Au-Sb/Bi cluster models to generate benchmark potential energy surfaces and hyperfine structure constants (A and B factors). The calculated nuclear quadrupole moments (Q) for Sb and Bi, as determined in our prior work, will be used to derive experimental-level coupling constants for validation. These benchmarks will then be used to parameterize a system-tailored, many-body dispersion correction (e.g., MBD@rsSCS) within a DFT framework. Finally, the calibrated functional will be deployed in periodic plane-wave DFT calculations to predict the band structure and density of states of one-dimensional aurophilic chains and two-dimensional networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitatively reliable electronic structure model for extended aurophilic systems, directly validated against molecular-scale quantum chemical accuracy. It will deliver predictive insights into the conductive and optical properties of metallophilic materials. Furthermore, it will establish a general calibration protocol using hyperfine properties as sensitive probes for weak interactions in heavy-element systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using nuclear quadrupole coupling constants—a direct probe of the electric field gradient—as the primary calibration target for DFT dispersion corrections, moving beyond total energy and geometry. This integrates Pyykkö's expertise in nuclear moments and relativistic atomic physics directly into the development of predictive solid-state models for weak bonding.

proposals.title: Isolating the Relativistic Signal in a Single Gold Atom: A Computational Foreground-Subtraction Approach
proposals.motivation: Relativistic effects are crucial for understanding the chemistry and physics of heavy elements like gold, governing properties from its color to its bonding. However, these effects are inherently entangled with non-relativistic quantum mechanics in any total observable. A method to isolate and quantify the pure relativistic contribution in a fundamental atomic system is lacking, hindering a foundational understanding of how these effects emerge at the single-atom scale.
proposals.hypothesis: The relativistic 'fingerprint' of a single gold atom can be isolated as a residual signal by computationally subtracting a high-accuracy non-relativistic description from a fully relativistic calculation of the same total atomic property.
proposals.method: We will perform a series of high-precision ab initio calculations for a single gold atom. For key properties (e.g., orbital energies, hyperfine structure A and B constants, electric field gradients at the nucleus), we will compute two values: one using a fully relativistic 4-component method (like Dirac-Hartree-Fock or Dirac-Coulomb) and one using a non-relativistic Schrödinger-based method with the same high-quality basis sets and correlation treatment (e.g., CCSD(T)). The relativistic contribution for each property is defined as the difference: δ_rel = Value(Relativistic) - Value(Non-relativistic). This systematic subtraction mimics the foreground-removal technique used in cosmology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, property-by-property decomposition of relativistic effects in a prototypical heavy atom, creating a benchmark dataset. It will establish a generalizable 'foreground-subtraction' methodology for isolating relativistic contributions, applicable to other heavy elements and molecular systems studied in the group.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the conceptual framework of signal isolation from astrophysics to relativistic quantum chemistry and applying it with rigorous computational control to the minimal system of a single atom, allowing for a clean separation never before achieved in such detail.

proposals.title: Mapping the Inverse Relativistic Regime: A High-Throughput Computational Screening for Destabilization in Heavy-Element Dimers and Clusters
proposals.motivation: Relativistic effects are universally known to strengthen bonds in heavy elements, primarily through the contraction and stabilization of s and p orbitals. This paradigm, however, overlooks scenarios where relativity may instead induce repulsion or destabilization, for instance via the expansion and energetic lowering of d or f orbitals that can enhance Pauli repulsion. Identifying such 'inverse relativistic' systems is crucial for a complete understanding of chemical bonding at the bottom of the periodic table and could explain anomalous structural or magnetic properties in actinide and superheavy element chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, high-throughput computational screening of heavy-element (Z > 70) systems will reveal distinct combinations of elements, oxidation states, and geometries where scalar and spin-orbit relativistic effects are the dominant cause of net interatomic repulsion or significant bond weakening.
proposals.method: We will design a library of candidate systems, including homo- and hetero-nuclear dimers and small clusters of 6p, 5f, and 6d block elements, varying formal oxidation states and spin multiplicities. Each candidate will undergo a two-step relativistic electronic structure analysis using the DIRAC code. First, a scalar relativistic Dirac-Kohn-Sham (DKS) calculation will be performed. Second, a full four-component DKS calculation including spin-orbit coupling will be executed. For each system, we will compute bonding energies with and without relativistic effects enabled, and perform a relativistic decomposition of orbital interactions using analysis tools like the relativistic version of the Energy Decomposition Analysis (EDA).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first classified 'map' of chemical systems where relativity inverts its traditional role, directly contributing to the fields of relativistic quantum chemistry and heavy-element synthesis. It will provide predictive guidelines for experimentalists targeting compounds with unusual bonding or potential metastability. Furthermore, the protocol itself establishes a general method for computationally screening for destabilizing relativistic effects.
proposals.novelty: While high-throughput screening is common in materials science, applying it specifically to isolate and catalog the *destabilizing* consequences of relativity—an effect typically associated with stabilization—is a novel inversion of the standard approach. The method integrates Pyykkö's deep expertise in relativistic effects on hyperfine structure and nuclear quadrupole moments with a systematic search strategy borrowed from quantum chemistry's study of anti-bonding interactions.

proposals.title: Benchmarking Nuclear Quadrupole Couplings in Ligated Gold Clusters via Fixed-Node Diffusion Quantum Monte Carlo
proposals.motivation: Gold clusters exhibit unique electronic and catalytic properties strongly influenced by relativistic effects and electron correlation. Pre-2026, high-accuracy quantum chemical methods like coupled-cluster are often intractable for these large, heavy-element systems, limiting the precision of property predictions. A robust benchmark for properties like electric field gradients (EFGs), crucial for interpreting nuclear quadrupole moments from molecular data, is therefore lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Fixed-Node Diffusion Quantum Monte Carlo (FN-DMC) can provide benchmark-quality electron correlation energies and EFGs for ligated gold clusters, revealing significant deviations from standard density functional theory (DFT) predictions and enabling a more accurate extraction of nuclear quadrupole moments for gold isotopes.
proposals.method: We will apply the FN-DMC method, using Slater-Jastrow trial wavefunctions with orbitals from relativistic DFT (e.g., ZORA-PBE). The core focus will be on calculating EFGs at gold nuclei in selected systems (e.g., Au20, Au(SR)18-). We will employ the CASINO or QMCPACK codes, utilizing state-of-the-art (pre-2026) techniques for non-local pseudopotentials (e.g., the Dirac-Fock pseudopotentials of Dolg and Stoll) to treat scalar-relativistic and spin-orbit effects. Statistical analysis of the DMC data will yield EFGs with quantified uncertainties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first set of high-accuracy, correlated EFG benchmarks for gold clusters, directly informing the calibration of DFT functionals for heavy elements. It will provide refined, system-dependent values for the nuclear quadrupole moment of key gold isotopes (e.g., 197Au), enhancing the interpretation of spectroscopic and hyperfine structure data.
proposals.novelty: While QMC is established in physics for solids, its systematic application to the chemically complex, relativistic gold cluster systems for precise EFG calculations is novel. This bridges Pyykkö's expertise in nuclear quadrupole moments and relativistic effects with the high-correlation accuracy of stochastic QMC methods.

proposals.title: A First-Principles Analytical Pair Potential for the Aurophilic Interaction from Scalar Relativistic and Electron Correlation Effects
proposals.motivation: The aurophilic attraction (Au...Au ~3 Å) is a cornerstone of gold chemistry and nanoscience, governing structure in complexes, clusters, and materials. Existing model potentials often rely on empirical fitting or simplified physical pictures, lacking a rigorous, transferable form derived purely from ab initio data for the diatomic. A precise, analytical pair potential from first principles is essential for predictive large-scale simulations in catalysis and nanomaterials design.
proposals.hypothesis: The aurophilic potential minimum and its long-range dispersion tail can be accurately reproduced by an analytical function whose parameters are determined by fitting to high-level ab initio curves for the Au₂ dimer, explicitly including scalar relativistic effects, spin-orbit coupling, and electron correlation.
proposals.method: We will compute the ground-state potential energy curve of Au₂ using coupled-cluster theory with singles, doubles, and perturbative triples (CCSD(T)) and multi-reference configuration interaction (MRCI). The calculations will employ large basis sets and incorporate scalar relativistic effects via the Douglas-Kroll-Hess Hamiltonian or effective core potentials (ECPs), with spin-orbit coupling treated perturbatively. The ab initio points will be fitted to a physically motivated analytical form (e.g., modified Buckingham-type or Tang-Toennies potential) capturing repulsion, attraction, and dispersion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a benchmark, transferable Au-Au pair potential for molecular dynamics and Monte Carlo simulations of gold-based systems. It will quantitatively decompose the contributions of relativity and correlation to the bond strength, offering a definitive reference for force-field development. The potential will be made publicly available for the computational chemistry community.
proposals.novelty: While Au₂ has been studied computationally, constructing a precise, ready-to-use analytical pair potential purely from first-principles data, without empirical adjustment to condensed-phase properties, represents a novel and necessary step towards predictive modeling. The explicit integration of Pyykkö's expertise in relativistic effects and nuclear quadrupole moments informs the treatment of gold's core electrons.

ideas.description: This research direction integrates the concept of context-dependent covalent radii with advanced computational refinement for heavy elements to develop a unified, ab initio model. The model will use high-level relativistic quantum chemistry methods to parameterize radii for superheavy elements as continuous functions of oxidation state, coordination number, and bond order, moving beyond fixed single- or double-bond values. This will generate a predictive, chemically-adaptive radius database that can accurately estimate molecular geometries and bonding trends for exotic elements in novel oxidation states or coordination environments not yet synthesized. The core advance is a transferable, first-principles model that predicts structural chemistry for the heaviest elements under variable conditions, directly supporting the design and interpretation of frontier experiments.

It develops a computational framework to generate anisotropic bond density profiles that decompose the total bond order into its σ, π, and δ components for metal-metal quadruple bonds. These profiles, parameterized for relevant elements and oxidation states, provide a physically accurate map of the bonding electron distribution. The resulting data set allows for predictive modeling of molecular geometries, steric interactions with ligands, and trends in bond strength across the periodic table, offering a more robust tool for inorganic synthesis and catalyst design.

ideas.title: Isolate and Probe the Relativistic 'Fingerprint' of a Single Gold Atom
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from astrophysics to systematically study relativistic effects in a single gold atom. In cosmology, the fundamental signal of the cosmic microwave background is extracted by removing contaminating foreground noise using multi-frequency observations. Analogously, this project proposes to isolate the relativistic 'fingerprint' of a single gold atom by designing experiments and calculations that first quantify the atom's total properties, then computationally subtract the contributions from established, non-relativistic quantum mechanics. The residual, purified signal would directly manifest the scale-down impact of relativistic effects—such as spin-orbit coupling and the Darwin term—on electronic structure, binding energies, and response functions, providing a foundational understanding of how these effects emerge at the ultimate limit of scaling down to a single atom.

ideas.title: Applying Quantum Chemistry's Destabilization Screening to Heavy-Element Systems
ideas.description: The original idea seeks systems where relativistic effects decrease attraction or increase repulsion, inverting their typical role in bonding. Drawing an analogy from quantum chemistry, where computational screening for destabilizing orbital interactions is a solved problem for predicting molecular stability. The analogous solution involves high-throughput analysis of orbital symmetries and occupancies to find 'anti-bonding' scenarios. This solution is adapted by creating a targeted relativistic computational screening protocol for heavy-element systems. The research would involve running high-level relativistic (e.g., Dirac-Kohn-Sham) calculations on a designed library of heavy-element dimers and clusters, systematically varying oxidation states, spin multiplicities, and geometries. The key output would be a classified set of systems where relativity is the dominant cause of repulsion or bond weakening, providing a 'map' of the inverse relativistic regime to guide experimental synthesis or further theoretical study of repelled systems.

The resulting potential energy surfaces and electronic coupling parameters are used to construct a validated, system-specific model Hamiltonian or to calibrate a dispersion-corrected DFT functional. This calibrated model is then deployed in periodic boundary conditions to predict the band structure, density of states, and transport properties of the extended aurophilic network, ensuring quantitative reliability from the molecular interaction up to the solid-state properties.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Effective Medium Theory' from Composite Materials to Covalent Bonding
ideas.description: This idea challenges the additivity of covalent radii by drawing an analogy from Effective Medium Theory in composite materials science. In composites, the macroscopic property of a mixture is non-additive and depends on microstructure and interactions. The analogous solution is to model the bonded atom pair A-B as a unique composite system. The adapted research idea is to develop a quantum-chemical 'Bond EMT' framework. In this model, the equilibrium bond length R(AB) emerges from a self-consistent calculation where each atom is embedded in the effective medium of the other's perturbed electron density, incorporating effects of charge transfer, polarization, and orbital hybridization, fundamentally moving beyond the r(A) + r(B) paradigm.

ideas.title: Deconstructing the Relativistic Components of Aurophilic Bonding Through Computational Scaling
ideas.description: This research uses advanced quantum chemistry to quantitatively dissect the role of relativity in aurophilic bonds, moving beyond a binary relativistic/non-relativistic debate. By performing calculations on benchmark Au(I) dimer systems with systematically scaled or disabled relativistic operators, it maps how mass-velocity, Darwin, and spin-orbit terms individually influence bond strength, equilibrium distance, and electron density. The outcome will be a ranked, energetic partitioning of relativistic effects, identifying which components are strictly necessary to reproduce the observed attraction and which have countervailing roles.

ideas.title: Solve the inverse problem: radii from complex spectra via seismic tomography principles
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse problem from geophysical seismic tomography to molecular spectroscopy. In seismology, the internal structure of the Earth is deduced from surface wave measurements via model-based inversion. Analogously, here we propose to deduce the fundamental structural parameter of covalent radii from the complex spectroscopic data (e.g., UV-Vis, IR, Raman) of an unknown coordination compound or cluster. The adapted method involves: 1) Creating a parameterized quantum chemical or empirical forward model that simulates spectra based on input atomic positions defined by a set of covalent radii. 2) Applying an inversion algorithm to systematically vary these radii within the model, minimizing the difference between the simulated and observed spectra. The output is the set of radii that provides the best-fit 'internal structure' for the observed spectral 'surface data,' solving the inverse problem indirectly.

ideas.title: Predict covalent bonding from machine-learned atomic electron density profiles
ideas.description: This approach abandons the concept of a scalar radius entirely. Instead, a neural network model is trained on quantum chemistry data to predict the spherically averaged radial electron density distribution for an atom in any given chemical environment, defined by its oxidation state, coordination, and neighbor identities. Bond lengths between two atoms are not assigned via tabulated radii, but are computed as the distance where their predicted interacting density profiles meet a physically justified criterion, such as a specific density isosurface or minimum of the density Laplacian. This directly links structure to the underlying electron density, providing a transferable, first-principles-informed framework for predicting geometries across diverse and unprecedented molecular and solid-state systems.

ideas.title: Quantifying Many-Body Aurophilicity via Real-Space, Sub-Atomic Electron Density Descriptors
ideas.description: This research direction develops a real-space, electron-density-based framework to rigorously test the pairwise additivity assumption in aurophilic interactions. By constructing continuous descriptors from quantum mechanical electron densities—such as localized orbital locators, electron localization function (ELF), or non-covalent interaction (NCI) indices at the sub-atomic scale—we can map the evolution of bonding character across a cluster. This allows the direct decomposition of the total interaction energy into additive two-body and genuine many-body terms, correlating them with specific, fine-grained features in the electron density distribution that are invisible to discrete bond-order models.

ideas.title: ML-Guided Inverse Design of Relativistic Mimics via Covalent Radius Mapping
ideas.description: This research direction develops a machine learning framework trained on quantum chemical data (including relativistic DFT calculations) to predict element- and oxidation-state-specific covalent radii. The core synergy is using this model inversely: by setting a target radius (e.g., the contracted radius of a gold(I) complex), the framework screens and proposes combinations of lighter, non-relativistic elements and ligand environments predicted to achieve the same effective bonding radius. This enables the rational design of 'relativistic mimics' by directly targeting the geometric outcome of relativistic effects, moving beyond trial-and-error to a predictive, property-driven discovery pipeline for novel catalytic and materials complexes.

Instead of refining radii from idealized diatomics, the parameters are derived from and validated against systems featuring diverse substituents, steric bulk, oxidation states, and bonding motifs relevant to experimental chemistry. This approach simultaneously solves the data scarcity for superheavy elements and the oversimplification of standard radii, yielding transferable parameters that can reliably predict molecular geometry, stability, and reactivity in realistic synthetic targets, such as organometallic catalysts or supramolecular assemblies containing these exotic nuclei.

ideas.title: Ensemble-Derived Statistical Radii from Quantum Chemical Conformational Landscapes
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the sensitivity flaw by deriving atomic radii from statistical ensembles of real molecules, not single structures. It uses automated computational workflows to generate and analyze thousands of diverse molecular conformations and their electron densities from ab initio calculations. Robust statistical methods are then applied to this ensemble data to extract probability distributions for each atom type's effective radius, complete with confidence intervals. This produces a set of chemically transferable, context-aware radii parameters that inherently encode the variability present in real chemical systems.

ideas.title: Adaptive Covalent Radii via a Continuous Symmetry Measure
ideas.description: This idea challenges the constant covalent radius assumption by adapting a solution from structural biology. In protein science, continuous symmetry measures (CSM) quantify deviations from ideal symmetry as a continuous variable, providing more nuanced structural descriptors than rigid classifications. The adapted research idea proposes to develop a similar continuous 'coordination symmetry measure' for atoms in chemical systems. This measure would be a function of the geometric arrangement and electronic character of the atom's bonded neighbors. It would then serve as the input to a parameterized function that outputs a context-dependent effective covalent radius, making atomic size a dynamic property that varies smoothly with oxidation state, coordination number, and the specific asymmetry of the local coordination environment, leading to more accurate molecular and solid-state models.

ideas.title: Derive radii from experimental molecular databases, not theory-first models
ideas.description: This idea inverts the primary data selection criteria by deriving radii from complex, real molecules instead of idealized model systems. The analogy comes from structural geology and seismology, where the internal structure of the Earth is not defined by a perfect theoretical sphere but is derived by inverting the complex, real data from seismic waves traveling through the heterogeneous planet. Adapting this solution, the research would treat extensive experimental molecular structure databases (like the Cambridge Structural Database) as the foundational observational data. By developing an inversion or statistical fitting procedure, one would derive sets of atomic radii that best reproduce the ensemble of real interatomic distances and molecular geometries across diverse chemical environments, thereby grounding a fundamental chemical parameter in empirical reality rather than theoretical idealism.

ideas.title: Develop improved functionals for gold cluster bonding using a multi-fidelity data integration approach
ideas.description: This idea proposes creating improved density functionals for weak dispersive and relativistic bonding in gold systems by adapting the multi-fidelity modeling paradigm from aerodynamics and CFD. In that field, accurate but costly high-fidelity simulation data is used to correct efficient low-fidelity models, creating hybrid tools optimal for design. Here, the analogous solution is to treat a standard, efficient density functional as the 'low-fidelity model.' Its errors for critical gold cluster properties will be corrected using a machine-learned potential, trained on a strategically sparse set of 'high-fidelity' data from highly accurate but prohibitively expensive wavefunction-based quantum chemistry methods. The resulting hybrid functional will be specifically tailored to capture dispersion and relativistic effects in gold systems with high accuracy at computational cost near that of standard DFT, bridging the fidelity gap in computational materials discovery.

ideas.title: Bayesian Probabilistic Radii for Partial and Resonance Bonding
ideas.description: This research direction develops a Bayesian framework to derive probabilistic covalent radii for bonds with fractional or resonance-stabilized bond orders. Instead of assigning a single radius to a given bond order, the method infers a posterior probability distribution for the radius from experimental structural data, explicitly quantifying the uncertainty inherent in defining a 'length' for delocalized or intermediate bonds. This allows for the prediction of molecular geometries with associated confidence intervals, directly linking structural uncertainty to electronic structure ambiguity. The approach provides a rigorous tool for analyzing borderline bonding situations in catalysis, organic intermediates, and materials, where traditional integer-bond-order models fail.

ideas.title: Topological Analysis of Quantum Chemical Bond Networks via QTAIM
ideas.description: This approach constructs chemically rigorous bond networks for molecules and solids by applying the Quantum Theory of Atoms in Molecules (QTAIM) to electron density calculations to identify and characterize all chemical bonds. The resulting network, with edges weighted by real-space bonding descriptors like electron density or bond order, serves as the input for topological data analysis methods, such as computing persistent homology across a filtration of bond strength thresholds. This directly links the topological invariants of the network (e.g., cycles, connectivity features) to electronic structure, enabling the discovery of robust, interpretable relationships between global bonding topology and macroscopic chemical properties like stability or reactivity.

ideas.title: Refine radii for heavier and exotic elements using a data-driven, transfer learning approach
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to improve the accuracy of double-bond radii for superheavy and exotic elements by adapting a solution from computational protein design: transfer learning. In that field, models pre-trained on vast datasets of known proteins are fine-tuned with specialized data to predict structures for novel targets. Here, we will develop a foundational machine learning model trained on high-fidelity quantum chemical data for lighter elements to understand fundamental bonding-radius relationships. This model will then be fine-tuned using sparse, computationally expensive calculations on superheavy elements, effectively transferring chemical intuition from known regions of the periodic table to predict refined radii for poorly characterized, heavier, and exotic species with greater accuracy and efficiency than direct computation alone.

ideas.title: Predicting Solid-State Properties via Covalent Radius Scaling Laws
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from astrophysics, where scaling laws (like the mass-radius relation for stars) predict complex bulk behavior from a single primary variable. The analogous problem in solid-state chemistry is predicting bulk properties from molecular-scale parameters. The solution is to derive 'covalent radius scaling laws'—empirical or semi-empirical power-law relationships (e.g., Property ∝ (Average Covalent Radius)^β) that connect the fundamental atomic-scale descriptor (covalent radius) to macroscopic solid-state properties like lattice constants, bulk modulus, or thermal expansion. By calibrating the scaling exponent β for different structure types or bonding classes, this approach would create a simple, interpretable framework to explain and predict trends across families of materials, scaling up from atomic dimensions to bulk behavior.

ideas.title: Bayesian Uncertainty-Quantified Electronic Structure for Aurophilic Networks
ideas.description: This research direction integrates Bayesian inference with solid-state Density Functional Theory (DFT) to study periodic systems featuring aurophilic contacts. A Bayesian model first fits experimental structural data (e.g., from crystallography) to obtain posterior probability distributions for key geometric parameters, such as Au...Au distances and angles, explicitly quantifying their uncertainty. These parameter distributions are then used to generate an ensemble of representative supercells, which are analyzed using high-throughput periodic DFT calculations. The final output is a statistically robust, uncertainty-quantified prediction of electronic band structure properties, directly linking the inherent structural disorder of aurophilic networks to the confidence intervals of their functional electronic behavior.

ideas.title: Topological Analysis of Non-Relativistic Contributions to Aurophilic Bond Networks
ideas.description: This research fuses the challenge to aurophilicity's purely relativistic explanation with topological data analysis (TDA) on bond networks. It applies TDA methods like persistent homology to computational datasets of gold clusters and extended structures, systematically comparing networks generated from relativistic versus non-relativistic quantum chemical calculations. The synergy lies in using the topological persistence of rings, cavities, and connection patterns as a direct, multi-dimensional fingerprint to isolate and characterize the structural role of non-relativistic contributions. This reveals whether these effects merely modulate known relativistic bonds or induce fundamentally different, topologically distinct connectivity.

ideas.title: Bayesian Transfer Learning for Robust Atomic Radii in Complex Chemical Environments
ideas.description: This research direction integrates a diverse dataset of molecules with complex substituent effects into a Bayesian inference framework. The synergy creates a model that learns probabilistic relationships between chemical descriptors (e.g., electronegativity, steric bulk) and the posterior distributions of atomic radii. This allows the model to predict not just a radius for an atom in a new, complex environment, but a full probability distribution that captures both the inherent uncertainty in the parameter and the model's confidence based on the similarity of the new environment to the training data. Consequently, the approach provides rigorously quantified, chemically intuitive predictions that are robust and interpretable when extrapolating to novel molecular systems beyond the original training set.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Docking Algorithms for Aurophilic Contact Prediction in Periodic DFT
ideas.description: This idea transfers a solution from computational structural biology to the original condensed matter problem. In protein-protein docking, the challenge of finding a stable binding interface between two large, complex biomolecules is solved by a multi-stage pipeline: rapid, coarse-grained sampling followed by detailed refinement. We adapt this to the study of extended periodic systems with aurophilic contacts. A pre-processing step uses a classical or semi-empirical potential, designed to capture the essential physics of aurophilic attraction, to sample and score relative orientations and registry of molecular or cluster subunits within a supercell. The most promising configurations, where close Au...Au contacts are predicted, are then used as the direct input for the solid-state DFT code. This hybrid approach leverages the speed of docking-like algorithms to navigate the vast configuration space of extended systems, ensuring the expensive periodic DFT calculations are performed only on structures highly likely to exhibit significant aurophilic interactions, thereby making the study of such systems more computationally tractable and systematic.

ideas.description: This system deduces element- and bond-specific covalent radii by jointly analyzing spectroscopic data and complementary quantum chemical inputs through a constrained machine learning architecture. It is trained on a vast, synthesized database of known molecular complexes with associated spectra and computed descriptors. Hard physical laws and bonding principles are embedded as inviolable constraints within the neural network's optimization process, preventing non-physical predictions. This approach transforms an ill-posed inverse problem into a well-regularized, data-driven mapping that yields robust, chemically meaningful radii estimates even for novel complexes.

ideas.title: Predict Covalent Radii via Graph Neural Networks, Inspired by Protein Folding
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from protein structure prediction, specifically the use of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), to the problem of predicting covalent radii. In computational biology, GNNs are successfully trained on databases of known protein structures to predict atomic coordinates by learning the complex interactions within a molecular graph. We adapt this by representing chemical systems as graphs where nodes are atoms and edges are bonds or interatomic interactions. The GNN model will be trained on quantum chemical or crystallographic datasets to learn the mapping from an atom's local chemical environment—including neighboring atom types, bond orders, and coordination geometry—to its context-dependent covalent radius, enabling accurate, transferable predictions for diverse and novel materials.

ideas.title: Validate aurophilic attraction in solution using biomolecular NMR-inspired solvent parameterization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution-validation paradigm from biomolecular NMR spectroscopy to the problem of aurophilicity. In structural biology, computational models of protein-ligand interactions are rigorously tested and refined against experimental NMR data collected in buffered aqueous solution, which accounts for the full complexity of the solvent environment. The adapted research idea involves developing quantum chemical/molecular dynamics models of aurophilic systems that are explicitly parameterized for solvent properties (dielectric constant, polarizability, donor/acceptor number). These models will then be tested against experimental data obtained from gold complexes in solution using techniques sensitive to weak, non-covalent interactions—such as diffusion-ordered NMR for assessing association or time-resolved spectroscopy for probing excited-state dynamics influenced by aurophilic contacts. The direct comparison in varied solvents will validate the theoretical models' ability to predict aurophilic attraction outside the gas phase, moving the field from theoretical prediction to experimentally grounded understanding in relevant chemical environments.

ideas.title: Develop and validate solvent-aware ML potentials for aurophilic interactions in gold clusters
ideas.description: This project integrates machine-learned potential energy surfaces (ML-PES) with experimental validation to study aurophilic interactions in realistic solvent environments. We will train ML potentials not just on gas-phase quantum chemistry data, but explicitly on clusters in implicit and explicit solvent models, targeting the accurate description of solvent-mediated Au...Au attractions. The experimental data will, in turn, be used to validate and iteratively refine the ML potentials, creating a synergistic cycle that yields both a robust computational tool and fundamental insight into how solvents control aurophilic bonding.

ideas.title: Applying Persistent Homology from Protein Folding to Covalent Bond Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts the application of persistent homology from computational biology's protein folding studies to the analysis of covalent bond networks. In protein folding, TDA methods like persistent homology are used on atom contact networks across distance scales to identify stable topological features that correspond to structural motifs. By directly analogizing this approach, we model atoms as nodes and use covalent radius relationships to build a filtered simplicial complex (where edges form between atoms based on a distance threshold derived from their covalent radii). As the filtration parameter increases (e.g., from 0.5x to 2.0x the summed covalent radii), we compute persistent homology to generate barcodes or persistence diagrams. The resulting multi-scale topological signatures—highlighting persistent cycles (rings), connected components (chains/clusters), or voids—provide a novel descriptor for bonding patterns. This can classify molecular or material structures, predict stable configurations from bonding topology, and identify regions where bonding deviates from expected geometry, offering a complementary tool to traditional chemical graph theory.

ideas.description: This research direction combines the need for solvent-aware models of aurophilicity with the tools of solid-state band structure calculation. The core idea is to use periodic DFT codes, adapted to include implicit or explicit solvent models, to simulate infinite chains or 2D networks with aurophilic contacts in a dielectric environment. This allows us to systematically study how solvent polarity, polarizability, and specific interactions (like with aromatic systems) not only affect the strength of individual Au...Au contacts but also modify the collective electronic structure, potentially opening or closing band gaps and shifting Fermi levels. The synergy enables the predictive design of solvent-responsive metallophilic materials whose conductivity, optical properties, or catalytic activity can be switched or tuned by the surrounding medium.

ideas.description: This research develops a robust machine learning potential for gold clusters by embedding a continuous uncertainty quantification mechanism directly into the molecular dynamics engine. The system employs an active learning protocol where configurations with high predictive uncertainty trigger targeted first-principles calculations, dynamically expanding the training dataset. This closed-loop approach ensures the ML model remains accurate and reliable even when simulating rare events or exploring previously unseen regions of the potential energy surface. The result is a self-improving potential capable of long-time, large-scale simulations with guaranteed fidelity to the underlying quantum mechanical physics.

The system then prioritizes synthesis and measurement for molecules where model uncertainty is highest or where substituent combinations represent under-explored, high-interaction regions of chemical parameter space. This ensures each new data point maximally improves model robustness and generalizability, directly targeting the model's weaknesses rather than merely increasing dataset size.

ideas.title: Adapt Bayesian Hierarchical Modeling from Ecology for Multi-Source Radius Uncertainty
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of Bayesian hierarchical modeling (BHM) from population ecology to the problem of quantifying uncertainties in radii from experimental data. In ecology, BHMs are used to fuse multiple, imperfect surveys (each with different detection biases) to estimate species abundance, coherently propagating all uncertainties. The adapted research idea applies this by constructing a BHM where the true physical radius is the latent parameter. Each independent experimental dataset (e.g., from X-ray diffraction, neutron scattering, or electron microscopy) is incorporated via a dedicated likelihood model that captures its unique error characteristics and calibration factors. Prior distributions are informed by theoretical predictions or previous measurements. Using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, the model infers a unified posterior probability distribution for the radius, providing a complete quantification of uncertainty that synthesizes evidence from all available, heterogeneous data sources.

ideas.title: Leveraging Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Chemical Substituent Space
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to solve the challenge of including more complex molecular environments by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, species distribution models (like MaxEnt) predict where a species can live by learning the relationship between known occurrence points and multi-dimensional environmental variables, efficiently mapping vast territories. Adapted to computational chemistry, we treat a target molecular property (e.g., reaction rate, binding affinity) as the 'species' and the substituent environment (characterized by steric, electronic, and topological descriptors) as the 'environmental niche.' By building such a model on an initial diverse dataset, we can predict property landscapes across substituent space. This predictive map identifies regions of high uncertainty or interesting predicted behavior, enabling a targeted, efficient expansion of the dataset beyond simple model systems to strategically sample the most complex and informative molecular environments.

ideas.title: Robust Bayesian Uncertainty Quantification with Prior Sensitivity Analysis and Model Selection
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach quantifies uncertainties in radii by embedding Bayesian inference within a robust validation framework. It begins with a baseline analysis using reference priors to minimize subjective input, then explicitly evaluates how the posterior distributions change across a comprehensive, physically-plausible space of prior assumptions. The framework integrates formal sensitivity metrics and Bayesian model comparison to distinguish between data-driven constraints and prior-induced beliefs. This process yields final uncertainty estimates that are explicitly validated for robustness, providing a more reliable and defensible quantification than a single Bayesian fit.
